entries.title: Lefty Matthew Boyd thrives for Cubs in chilly weather vs. Angels at Wrigley
entries.summary: <p>Except for the man in shirt sleeves standing in the middle of the diamond Wednesday afternoon, Wrigley Field looked like a winter clearance sale at Eddie Bauer.</p><p>Parkas, blankets, ski masks, thermals, gloves, scarves, a fur coat or two (we see you, Marquee’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/03/26/taylor-mcgregor-keli-mcgregor-marquee-sports-network-espn-chicago-cubs-colorado-rockies-alanna-rizzo-charissa-thompson-brian-griese-mike-mccarthy" >Taylor McGregor</a>) — all making a mockery of a calendar that had just flipped to April. Game time temperature, 39 degrees, with a 21 mph wind blowing toward home plate.</p><p>And then there was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> left-hander Matthew Boyd, acting blissfully unaffected by conditions that had a crowd of 25,125 hunkered down in miserable solidarity. Every player on both teams sported a long-sleeve T-shirt under his uniform jersey. Everyone except Boyd.</p><p>“It’s like, what are we doing here?’’ said his batterymate, Carson Kelly.</p><p>But in this battle of man vs. the elements, the man won.</p><p>“I know he’s from the Northwest [Mercer Island, Washington], and it seemed to work for him,’’ Kelly said after Boyd struck out 10 and allowed only two hits (both to Angels shortstop Zach Neto) and two runs (one earned) in 5⅔ innings in the Cubs’ 6-2 victory in the rubber match of this three-game series.</p><p>But it did beg the question: If Boyd was willing to forgo an extra layer of clothing when the temperature was 39 degrees and the wind chill subtracted another 10 degrees, how cold would it have to be before he opted for a little extra warmth?</p><p>“Good question,’’ Kelly said. “Have to ask him. It might have to be in the negative range, and that might not even do it. I wonder what would happen if it’s 90 degrees and he has no undershirt. I guess we’ll have to see.’’</p><p>Boyd, you’ll be relieved to know, had added a long-sleeve shirt to his ensemble after the game.</p><p>“It’s just preference,’’ he said, “no more than that, right?’’</p><p>Boyd said he can recall wearing sleeves only once in his big-league career, when the Tigers opened the season (where else) in Chicago against the White Sox.</p><p>“I don’t remember how cold it was,’’ he said. “I think it was below 30, and I wore sleeves. I just thought it was a weird thing. I never really wore much in high school when I was pitching.’’</p><p>Boyd was way off, by the way, in his recollection of how cold it was on the South Side that day. A check of the box score shows it was 48 degrees, with 20 mph-plus winds that matched Wednesday’s North Side howlers.</p><p>But if short sleeves can produce these types of results, maybe Boyd should consider wearing Speedos under his uniform. He gave up a single to Neto to lead off the game, then promptly picked him off, his 12th successful pickoff since coming to the Cubs last season. That ranks second in club history to Hippo Vaughn, who has 17 pickoffs, but it took the crafty Hippo nine seasons to reach that number.</p><p>Boyd then got 16 of the next 17 Angels out, Neto’s walk in the fourth the only spoiler, striking out four in a row before Craig Counsell lifted him after Neto’s two-out single and a walk to Mike Trout. Jo Adell’s RBI single off Hunter Harvey brought home a run, and an error on a ball that skipped past third baseman Alex Bregman brought home another, but Harvey caught old friend Yoan Moncada looking for an inning-ending strikeout.</p><p>“That was huge,’’ Boyd said. “Getting that final out and then going back out for another inning after that, that was awesome, especially in that weather, you know.</p><p>“We just played really well all around. It was awesome.’’</p><p>For the record, Boyd is not the first pitcher to brave the cold underdressed. Any Cubs fan with a memory surely can recall others. Shoot, only one night earlier, as Counsell noted, Jose Soriano of the Angels held the Cubs scoreless.</p><p>“Maybe short sleeves is the way to go,’’ said Counsell, whose team broke even in this season-opening six-game homestead.</p><p>“I mean, short sleeves didn’t give up too many runs this series.’’</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Seiya Suzuki to begin his rehab in Double-A Knoxville on Friday
entries.summary: <p>Cubs outfielder Seiya Suzuki, who has missed the first six games with a sprained right knee, will work out with Double-A Knoxville on Thursday, then begin a rehab assignment with the Smokies when they open their season Friday night.</p><p>Manager Craig Counsell said Suzuki will play the outfield with Knoxville and expects him to be gone no more than a week before he rejoins the big-league club.</p><p>“Probably three to seven days, somewhere in that range,’’ Counsell said. “Seeing how he feels while he’s playing, then go from there.’’</p><p>The Cubs open a three-game series with the Guardians in Cleveland on Friday, followed by a three-game set against the Rays in St. Petersburg.</p><p>Suzuki has been out since March 14, when he hurt the knee on a stolen base in the first inning of Japan’s quarterfinal loss to Venezuela in the World Baseball Classic in Miami. In his absence, Counsell has used Matt Shaw four times and Michael Conforto twice. Shaw, who played third base last season, handled three chances cleanly Wednesday and had two singles, driving in three runs.</p><h3>Tough lesson for rookie skipper</h3><p>Kurt Suzuki, who caught 16 seasons in the big leagues but had no managing experience before the Angels hired him this season, learned a tough lesson Wednesday. Time waits for no man, especially a rookie manager.</p><p>Suzuki wanted to challenge a close play at the plate in the third inning. Plate umpire David Rackley had ruled that Miguel Amaya’s headfirst slide beat Zach Neto’s relay throw on Nico Hoerner’s double to the left-center-field gap. But umpiring crew chief Chris Guccione ruled that Suzuki did not signal he wanted a challenge in the 15 seconds allotted to make that decision.</p><p>Guccione explained his call to pool reporter Jordan Cohen of the Associated Press.</p><p>“When a close play happens like that, the manager is required to immediately ‘hold,’ to signal to start the clock, which is 15 seconds,’’ Guccione said.</p><p>“Once I see the manager hold by raising his hand, I’ll radio up to the press box to the [scoreboard operator Rick Fuhs] up there, who then starts the clock. By then, they have 15 seconds to wave [the challenge] off, challenge, whatever they want to do.’’</p><p>Guccione said the umpires wear a belt loaded with technological gizmos that include a buzzer, which goes off when the time expires. He said he was watching Suzuki when the buzzer sounded, and zeros also registered on his belt.</p><p>“Kurt came up a little late,’’ he said. “We want to get the play right, but there are regulations we need to follow.’’</p><p>The play was pivotal in the outcome of the game. Amaya’s run was the first in a four-run rally, as the Cubs followed Hoerner’s double with four more hits. Neto also committed a throwing error, and there was a sacrifice fly.</p><p>Hoerner, who’s hitting .318, had two doubles and a single and added his fourth stolen base, which made him, at least momentarily, the big-league leader. Suzuki lost a challenge on that call.</p><h3>Hard day</h3><p>The Angels had a rough afternoon. Mike Trout dropped a fly ball in a wind-challenged center field, and first baseman Jeimer Candelario let a pop fly fall just a couple of feet away from him.<br></p>
entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: 8-year-old child, man and woman shot in Douglas
entries.summary: <p>Three people, including an 8-year-old, were shot in Douglas Wednesday afternoon, according to Chicago police.</p><p>A man, woman and child were in the 3400 block of South Martin Luther King Jr. Drive at 4:48 p.m. when a dark SUV approached and at least one person inside started shooting, police said. The child, 8, was hit in her foot and taken to Comer Children’s Hospital in good condition.</p><p>The woman, 28, was shot in her knee and taken to University of Chicago Medical Center in good condition, police said. The man, 24, was shot in the arm, chest and groin and was taken to Stroger Hospital, also in good condition.</p><p>No one is in custody.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 18-year-old fatally shot in Englewood
entries.summary: <p>An 18-year-old was killed after being shot by four people in Englewood Wednesday afternoon, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man was inside a business in the 6600 block of South Wentworth Avenue at 4:18 p.m. when four people with handguns exited a vehicle outside and shot him, police said. He was taken to University of Chicago Medical Center, where he died.</p><p>No one is in custody.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Josh Giddey sits against Pacers and Bulls will be careful with his return
entries.summary: <p><b>Josh Giddey </b>was held out Wednesday against the Pacers with tightness in his left hamstring, the same one that had cost him 19 games earlier this season. Coach <b>Billy Donovan </b>said Giddey is day-to-day.</p><p>Whether Giddey will play again this season is up in the air.</p><p>Donovan described how the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> must weigh players’ desire to play with their safety. One example is forward/center <b>Jalen Smith</b>, who reaggravated his right calf during the Bulls’ March 25 game in Philadelphia. The Bulls could have ramped up Smith’s recovery and potentially gotten him back for the last couple of games but decided against it, shutting him down a day after the injury.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/01/josh-giddey-gets-big-minutes-rewards-bulls-with-triple-double" >Giddey</a> won’t be any different. It sounds like his health, and not lottery odds, will determine his next action.</p><p>“If they’re healthy, [the players] want to play,” Donovan said before the Bulls’ 145-126 loss to Indiana. “I’ve heard nothing from anybody that we would hold guys out unless it was for a medical reason or we were concerned about something potentially going into the offseason that would linger on and be a problem.”</p><p>The Bulls’ problem of a season is one game closer to ending after Wednesday’s loss, completing the Pacers’ sweep of the teams’ season series. Indiana compiled 49 assists and led by 17 at halftime.</p><p>Center/forward <b>Guerschon Yabusele</b> scored 20 points to lead the Bulls, who dropped their fifth straight and fell to 29-47.</p><p>“The situation is what it is right now,” Yabusele said.</p><h2><b>Jones’ jump</b></h2><p>If the Bulls were still in contention for even a play-in berth, guard <b>Tre Jones </b>would be peaking at the right time. Instead, Jones is just trying to finish strong for a team whose season is ending April 12.</p><p>Before then, Jones and his teammates are working to hone their identity as a team that plays fast and make strides on defense.</p><p>“It’s still a dream of ours to wake up and play basketball every day,” Jones said. “We’re all extremely driven still. We don’t need any other motivators [or] anything else to keep us going.”</p><p>Prior to Wednesday, Jones was surging over the Bulls’ past six games. During that stretch, Jones scored at least 15 points in each contest, averaging 18.8.</p><p>He also managed to distribute to his teammates, giving out 5.7 assists per game.</p><p>“Just trying to continue to stay aggressive,” Jones said. “A lot of times I am looking to pass first, so that sometimes means I’m passing up on shots. Continue to just try to take those shots and know that it will open up passes for my teammates, knowing that the defense knows I want to pass first.”</p><p>Jones’ shot, however, has been dangerous.</p><p>Before the game, Jones was shooting a career-best 54.8% from the floor. That’s helped him lead the team with 5.0 win shares and an offensive rating of 129.</p><p>“I’m trying to impact winning as best as I can, both on the offensive side and defensive side and continue to try to become a better leader for our group,” Jones said.</p><h2>Briefly</h2><p>Center <b>Nick Richards </b>(sprained right elbow) was out. Donovan said Richards is progressing but still having elbow issues.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: As questions linger, Bulls trying to move on from Jaden Ivey saga
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> guard Tre Jones is not surprised by how much attention the Jaden Ivey situation has received since he was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/30/league-source-says-bulls-working-exit-strategy-for-jaden-ivey" >cut loose Monday</a>.</p><p>“I feel like that’s part of the business,” Jones said. “The media’s always looking for something to talk about. This is definitely easy to talk about; you don’t see it every day, someone talking about their religion so much in this space. It is what it is and we’re just ready to move on.”</p><p>That might be what Jones wants, but there are still lingering questions. Some of the most pressing are what did the Bulls know about Ivey’s conduct before acquiring him, how in-depth was their vetting process and what does the entire saga say about their judgment.</p><p>As he is frequently, Bulls coach Billy Donovan was forced Wednesday to address the story instead of executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/31/jaden-ivey-fiasco-sheds-light-bulls-failing-front-office" >the man who actually made the trade</a>. Donovan, who said he has been included with player background in the past, was not involved in gathering information on Ivey and finding out what he was like with the Pistons.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/31/jaden-ivey-fiasco-sheds-light-bulls-failing-front-office" >Jaden Ivey fiasco sheds more light on Bulls’ failing front office</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/30/league-source-says-bulls-working-exit-strategy-for-jaden-ivey" >Bulls waive guard Jaden Ivey after anti-Pride remarks</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>And there’s a good reason for that: Donovan was on the road and too busy onboarding new players after a chaotic trade deadline, preparing for a game in Canada against the Raptors.</p><p>“As a coach, you want to trust what’s going on back in Chicago, and I want them to be able to trust what’s going on in Toronto,” Donovan said. “You kind of all have to work together and do a job, so to speak.</p><p>“I don’t have all the intel on what wasn’t said prior to his arrival. All I was trying to do at that point and time was, how do I get this guy acclimated to our team?”</p><p>Ivey only played four games with the Bulls prior to getting shut down for the season March 26 to manage left knee pain. During those four appearances, Ivey’s athleticism and burst were clearly diminished from what he was before breaking his leg and suffering a knee injury with Detroit.</p><p>Those were only some of the issues with Ivey: others surfaced in his social media outbursts, including rants about his religious views and Pride, the latter costing him his job.</p><p>“He wasn’t around the group a whole lot, just with the injuries that he was dealing with,” Jones said. “We didn’t really get to get really close with him off the court or on the court really, just because he wasn’t around that much. Obviously, it’s unfortunate, everything that happened. We wish him the best.”</p><p>At the tail end of a lost season, the Bulls are facing another suboptimal situation. They’ve weathered losing and a teardown, but those are more standard parts of the NBA life.</p><p>This is different. They’ve been written and talked about in places a lottery-bound team usually isn’t, and a former teammate has become a subject of national speculation and curiosity.</p><p>Still, the Bulls are trying to keep moving forward to get something valuable from the last few games on their schedule. Jones indicated the Bulls are doing well to tune out the buzz coming from the Ivey situation.</p><p>“I wouldn’t say it’s really hovering over the team at all,” Jones said. “We’re continuing to get closer and closer off the court and on the court as a team. We continue to focus just on the game, on what’s in front of us. Obviously, it’s unfortunate what is going on but we continue to stay locked in on the things that we can control and hopefully just getting better as a group.”</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Chicagoan helps launch Artemis II, NASA's first moon mission in 53 years
entries.summary: <p>Amy Lendian, a Chicago resident, remembered watching Apollo 8 lift off in 1968 as she shook off nerves heading to work on Wednesday morning.</p><p>She was set to enter the control room at the Kennedy Space Center for what could have been a 16-hour shift. Her mind was on the mission from 58 years ago, which helped turn her dreams toward the stars.</p><p>Now 67, and the acting systems engineer manager for the Artemis II lunar orbital mission and former fire suppression and console engineer for the Artemis I rocket, she was thinking back on that 9-year-old girl who watched "almost the same" mission she would play a part in Wednesday.</p><p>“As a kid I wanted to be an astronaut,” Lendian said. “ So for me this has a very special meaning.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ee0000" name="image-ee0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Chicagoan Amy Lendian, acting systems engineer manager for the Artemis II lunar orbital mission, and former fire suppression and console engineer for the Artemis I rocket." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a4e750a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/919x516+0+49/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2F6a%2F61b14407401cb889db0d4483a71b%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-46-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9800e3b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/919x516+0+49/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2F6a%2F61b14407401cb889db0d4483a71b%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-46-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a4e750a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/919x516+0+49/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2F6a%2F61b14407401cb889db0d4483a71b%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-46-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicagoan Amy Lendian, acting systems engineer manager for the Artemis II lunar orbital mission, and former fire suppression and console engineer for the Artemis I rocket.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lendian was called out of retirement to helm a team of four engineers in the control room monitoring systems that range from water and electrical to fire suppression in case of an emergency — a much larger task with an actual crew onboard. Artemis I was unmanned.</p><p>Artemis II is the opening shot of NASA’s grand plans for a permanent moon base. Three Americans and one Canadian are set to fly around the moon without stopping or even orbiting — then head straight back for a Pacific splashdown. They will set a new distance record for the farthest humans have traveled from Earth as they zoom some 4,000 miles (6,400 kilometers) beyond the moon and then hang a U-turn.</p><p>The rocket, destined for the moon, took off shortly after 5:30 p.m. without issue, starting the astronauts' 10-day journey — the farthest any humans have traveled from Earth.</p><p>NASA had not launched a moon mission since Apollo 17 in 1972. The space program is aiming to land near the lunar south pole in 2028.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5a0000" name="image-5a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c1206c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bebf30c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf088f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Calvin Berney (left) and Sommer Von Behren (right) wait for the Artemis II launch at the Adler Planetarium on Wednesday, April 1, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf088f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8dc32ef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf088f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F83%2F7a%2Fe41b46b440639eec0806119123b6%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Calvin Berney (left) and Sommer Von Behren (right) wait for the Artemis II launch at the Adler Planetarium on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Michelle Nichols, the Adler Planetarium's senior director of public programs, worked at the Kennedy Space Center's space camp in the early 90s and has witnessed launches firsthand. She was always in awe of the low rumble that resonates in everyone's chest as the rocket lifts off, she said, but that the gravity of the watching the current mission was historic.</p><p>"Most of the people on this planet weren't alive the last time humanity was in the vicinity of the moon," Nichols, who herself was two months old in 1972 when Apollo 17 orbited the moon. "It's difficult to quantify the excitement."</p><p>Dozens of people gathered in the planetarium's cafe, counting down with Nichols before erupting into cheers, and some tears, as the rocket lifted off into the sky.</p><p>Among those watching the launch at the Adler was Viviene Ebel of Ottawa, Illinois, and her 5-year-old brother, Adler. Like Lendian years ago, Viviene dreams of working at NASA. </p><p>Wednesday was Viviene's second rocket launch; she and her family watched a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket launch in December. That inspired her to do a science project for her class about Artemis.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f10000" name="image-f10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/97c30d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/31db4f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8546a27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Viviene Ebel, 8, cheers as the Artemis II rocket launches Wednesday, April 1, 2026, at the Adler Planetarium. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8546a27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d8f4e30/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8546a27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F4e%2F82f579a540ffbdbd067c8f707b8f%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Viviene Ebel, 8, cheers as the Artemis II rocket launches Wednesday. She watched the launch at the Adler Planetarium. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Viviene also hopes to one day walk on her favorite planet — Mars. She's interested in that planet because it may have been habitable, once upon a time.</p><p>"I just hope I get there," Ebel said.</p><p>With the rocket firing toward the moon, Lendian said her day would end with beans and cornbread, a longtime NASA tradition.</p><p>But she knows the impact of a delayed launch all too well, as Artemis I delays also delayed her wedding by several months. Lendian and her wife — Anne Barela, a former NASA engineer herself who worked on the agency’s social team during the Artemis I launch — ultimately tied the knot at the space center, taking their pictures in the Rocket Garden.</p><p>“We couldn’t plan anything because we weren’t sure when the rocket would fire,” Lendian said. So when they had the chance, after another delay, they had to hurry. "I called up Anne and said ‘Let’s put together a wedding,’ and we did it in six weeks. ... It was wonderful."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5b0000" name="image-5b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f1c850/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c50c8a6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dfeec7b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Amy Lendian took a selfie with the Artemis I rocket before that launch in November 2022. She was the fire suppression and console engineer for that mission." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dfeec7b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e2dd844/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dfeec7b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/401x225+0+149/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F7e%2Fad583ca943f7a9f003844b23bf04%2Fscreen-shot-2026-04-01-at-10-44-07-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Amy Lendian took a selfie with the Artemis I rocket before that launch in November 2022. She was the fire suppression and console engineer for that mission, and also is working on the Artemis II mission.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lendian had been a fire suppression systems engineer who landed at Florida's Kennedy Space Center in 2020. But last year, she and Barela, both trans women, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/03/12/trans-people-facing-gender-affirming-care-bans-flee-to-illinois-shield-law-states" target="_blank" ><u>moved to Chicago</u></a> amid a rash of anti-trans laws that made them feel unsafe.</p><p>With the launch taking place the same week as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2026/03/29/basketball-tournament-makes-space-for-trans-athletes-amid-international-sports-bans" target="_blank" ><u>Transgender Day of Visibility</u></a>, she said it was “a big deal.”</p><p>“All my trans siblings can do whatever they want to and be a part of something important like this," Lendian said.</p><p><i>Contributing: AP</i></p><div entries.title: Horoscope for Thursday, April 02, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Caution! Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions after 3:30 a.m. for the rest of the day. The moon is in Libra.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> The moon is opposite your sign today, increasing your focus on your most personal relationships. Be careful that you don't act unconsciously, without thinking. Guard against jealousy and possessiveness. Avoid knee-jerk reactions. (These are the people you care about.)</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a tricky day because you might have to put the interests or welfare of someone else before your own. This doesn't mean you're being a martyr — it's just what's happening. Consider this an opportunity to practice generosity and patience. Major brownie points!</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today it's easy to feel in harmony with yourself and to be able to do whatever you have to do. It's a good time for any kind of group activity. Physically, you have strong feelings of vitality, which will encourage playfulness and a desire to socialize.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> If possible, you’ll prefer to retire by yourself to your own private place today because you need to contemplate your navel. Take time to relax. You might also have a chance to observe how much your habits and past conditioning control your days.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you have a strong urge to speak to someone because you need to be heard. Your challenge, of course, is to find someone who will take a moment to genuinely listen to you. Bide your time and look around you because you will find someone — maybe even a stranger.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today it's easy to identify with what you own, which is why you might be reluctant to lend something to someone. (We all have days like this.) You might have to defend yourself about something that is really not that important. (Kind of weird.)</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon is in your sign, which makes you more emotional than usual. However, it will slightly increase your good luck over all the other signs, which is good news for you. This means things will tend to go your way. Why not test this out and ask the universe for a favor? Try it.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Solitude and the enjoyment of your own privacy will give you pleasure today because you need to take a moment to catch your breath. You're working hard now, and you're also socializing. Take this day to refuel and regenerate so you are replenished.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day to take a moment to talk to a friend or perhaps a member of a group and share your hopes and dreams with this person. Feedback from someone else about what you want could help you solidify future goals.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're high-visibility today, which means people notice you more than usual. In fact, today some people will know personal details about your private life. It's important that you know this, in case you have to do some damage control.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you want a bit of adventure! You need some stimulation. Therefore, do what you can to break free from your usual routine. Travel or explore your own city. Go someplace different. Visit new restaurants, new stores, new neighborhoods. Learn something! Meet new people.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, your emotional experiences are more intense than usual, especially in your encounters with others. You might also feel possessive about property, which is why you'll feel happy to be among familiar things while you ponder memories from your past.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Christopher Meloni (1961) shares your birthday today. You are strong-willed and direct. You are a generous idealist. This is a year of teaching and learning for you. Take time to renew your spiritual or religious beliefs. Explore philosophies that give you a better self-awareness and bring you closer to the true meaning of your life.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Man arrested after standoff in Garfield Park neighborhood
entries.summary: <p>Charges were pending Thursday for a man who holed himself up inside a home following a domestic incident on the West Side. </p><p>A SWAT team responded to the 2700 block of West Flournoy Street around 11:40 p.m. Wednesday for a man who barricaded himself after a domestic incident, Chicago police said. </p><p>The man, 23, was arrested without incident and charges were pending, police said. </p><p>No injuries were reported. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Imposing hefty taxes on nicotine patches won't improve public health
entries.summary: <p>By <a class="Link" href="https://nypost.com/2026/02/26/us-news/biz-groups-push-hochul-to-reverse-bro-tax-proposal-on-nicotine-pouches/" target="_blank" >pushing</a> a 75% wholesale tax on nicotine pouches, New York State Budget Director Blake Washington <a class="Link" href="https://13wham.com/news/local/a-distinction-without-a-difference-hochul-admin-proposes-tax-on-nicotine-pouches-zyn-governor-new-york-blake-washington-budget-director-public-health-smoking-philip-morris-international" target="_blank" >says</a>, Gov. Kathy Hochul is trying to address "a public health concern." That rationale is absurd on its face, since this tax would sharply raise the cost of a nicotine product that is far less hazardous than cigarettes, perversely <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/03/27/less-than-50-percent-of-new-yorkers-support-hochuls-nicotine-pouch-tax/" target="_blank" >discouraging</a> smokers from making a switch that could save their lives.</p><p>Hochul, who seems determined to portray a money grab as a benevolent intervention, is either oblivious or indifferent to the health consequences of taxing nicotine patches at the same rate as cigarettes. </p><p>"We see it as a distinction without a difference," Washington <a class="Link" href="https://13wham.com/news/local/a-distinction-without-a-difference-hochul-admin-proposes-tax-on-nicotine-pouches-zyn-governor-new-york-blake-washington-budget-director-public-health-smoking-philip-morris-international" target="_blank" >told</a> reporters in January.</p><p>That position ignores the huge difference between inhaling tobacco smoke, which <a class="Link" href="https://www.fda.gov/tobacco-products/health-effects-tobacco-use/chemicals-tobacco-products-and-your-health" target="_blank" >contains</a> myriad toxins and carcinogens, and orally absorbing nicotine from a pouch placed between the lip and gums. Hochul's framing also contradicts what the Food and Drug Administration said four days before the end of the Biden administration, when it <a class="Link" href="https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-authorizes-marketing-20-zyn-nicotine-pouch-products-after-extensive-scientific-review" target="_blank" >authorized</a> the marketing of Zyn nicotine pouches in two doses and 10 flavors.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-aa0000" name="module-aa0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>That decision was based on the FDA's determination that "the new products offer greater benefits to population health than risks." The data, said Matthew Farrelly, director of the Office of Science at the FDA's Center for Tobacco Products, "show that these nicotine pouch products meet that bar by benefiting adults who use cigarettes and/or smokeless tobacco products and completely switch to these products."</p><p>Nicotine pouches contain "substantially lower amounts of harmful constituents than cigarettes," the FDA noted. They therefore <a class="Link" href="https://www.fda.gov/tobacco-products/health-effects-tobacco-use/relative-risks-tobacco-products#NewNic" target="_blank" >offer</a> "a lower-risk alternative for adults who smoke cigarettes."</p><p>How much lower? To give you a sense of the difference, the Royal College of Physicians <a class="Link" href="https://www.rcp.ac.uk/resources/nicotine-without-smoke-tobacco-harm-reduction/" target="_blank" >estimates</a> that "the hazard to health" from electronic cigarettes, which likewise do not contain tobacco or burn anything but do require inhalation, "is unlikely to exceed 5% of the harm from smoking tobacco."</p><p>Nicotine pouches "contain far, far fewer harmful constituents compared to traditional tobacco products," <a class="Link" href="https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-health-effects-of-fda-authorized-zyn-nicotine-pouches/" target="_blank" >notes</a> Mary Hrywna, a tobacco control specialist at the Rutgers School of Public Health. The FDA's Zyn decision implicitly acknowledged that nicotine pouches are "much safer than cigarettes," <a class="Link" href="https://yonkerstimes.com/dont-tax-nicotine-pouches-they-helped-me-stop-smoking/" target="_blank" >says</a> Ray Niaura, a professor at New York University's School of Global Public Health.</p><p>Does that sound like "a distinction without a difference"? Washington conceded that cigarettes pose a much more serious cancer risk than nicotine pouches do but added that "there's an addictive property to both," which is "really the thrust behind" the proposed tax.</p><p>Seriously? The central problem with cigarettes is not their addictiveness, which would be of much less moment if smokers did not face a substantial danger of premature death. Other things being equal, a nicotine habit that promises to dramatically reduce that hazard surely counts as a big improvement.</p><p>The Hochul administration also argues that a hefty tax will deter teenagers from using nicotine pouches. But according to the National Youth Tobacco Survey, just <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/2025-National-Youth-Tobacco-Survey-Codebook-Version-2-28-26.pdf#page=551" target="_blank" >1.6% </a> of middle and high school students reported past-month use of nicotine pouches last year, down from <a class="Link" href="https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/73/wr/mm7341a2.htm?s_cid=mm7341a2_w" target="_blank" >1.8% in 2024</a>.</p><p>Since selling nicotine pouches to anyone younger than 21 is already illegal, enforcing that rule seems like a more sensible way to prevent underage consumption than imposing a new financial burden on adults. That is especially true given the evidence that nicotine pouches are an appealing alternative for adults who smoke.</p><p>A 2025 <a class="Link" href="https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2838608" target="_blank" >study</a>, for instance, found that "daily nicotine pouch use is most prevalent among adults who recently quit using another tobacco product." A <a class="Link" href="https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/static/searchtobacco/ZYN/PMTA_TPL_PM593-PM612_Zyn_01_13_2025_Redacted.pdf" target="_blank" >study</a> considered by the FDA found that a quarter of people who initially used Zyn in combination with cigarettes, smokeless tobacco, or both, had switched entirely to the nicotine pouches within 10 weeks.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://academic.oup.com/ntr/article/26/9/1150/7623369?login=false" target="_blank" >randomized trial</a> reported in 2024 found that nicotine pouches were especially effective at reducing cigarette consumption among low-income smokers, who would be disproportionately affected by Hochul's tax. These findings suggest that levy would <a class="Link" href="https://reason.org/commentary/new-york-gov-hochuls-nicotine-pouch-tax-would-be-bad-for-public-health/" target="_blank" >undermine</a> public health in the name of protecting it.</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e20000" name="module-e20000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Dear Abby: Woman rejects well-intentioned offers of support
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband slipped, fell and broke his hip. As I write this, he is having a total hip replacement. Family and friends have offered to "come and sit with me." I have been polite (when I really wanted to scream) and told them "No, thank you." Then they rephrase it and tell me they are coming. When I say "No," they forget that "No" is a complete sentence.</p><p>My husband wasn't in a car accident. It's not life-threatening. It's not a crisis situation. He simply fell and broke his hip. These days, hip replacements are routine procedures. I don't need a chaperone to sit with me. I know that everyone is different. I just wish people would ask if I wanted them to sit with me instead of TELLING me they're going to come.</p><p>I am a 62-year-old woman (who sometimes has an explosive temper). Everyone deals with this in their own way. I am content being by myself through this. Please remind people that, although they may mean well, they should ASK if someone wants company, instead of TELLING them they do. I think projecting one's feelings onto someone else is wrong. Do you agree? — <i>WIFE IN THE WAITING ROOM</i></p><p><b>DEAR WIFE:</b> Everyone is different. While you may think your husband's hip replacement is a minor procedure, some folks worry that no surgery is without risk. The individuals who volunteer to come and sit with you may do it because they want to be with you in case something goes wrong. While I agree it's a mistake to project one's feelings onto someone else, please control your "sometimes explosive" temper and remember there's a way to politely refuse an offer that's well intended.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I've been in a long-distance relationship for almost six years, engaged for two, and I love my fiance. But the thought of moving out west after spending so much time on the East Coast is really intimidating. It means leaving my parents behind, especially with my dad being sick in the hospital for almost a year and a half, and my mother struggling with health issues and obesity. My brother has alcohol issues and a history of seizures, and I worry about him, too.</p><p>At the same time, I feel so happy when I'm with my partner, and the idea of ending things with him would devastate me. I'm almost 40, and the weight of this decision is overwhelming. The anxiety has me torn between pursuing my happiness with him and staying close to my family. Do you have any advice? — <i>AT A CROSSROADS IN NEW YORK</i></p><p><b>DEAR 'CROSSROADS':</b> I do have some. At almost 40, if you haven't been able to convince your mother and brother to adopt healthier lifestyles, the chances are nil that they'll change. Consult a licensed psychologist for help to overcome your anxiety (and possibly some guilt) about doing something that's good for you by joining your fiance out west. Live your own life rather than engage in a fruitless fantasy that these relatives will change theirs if you stay.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For an excellent guide to becoming a better conversationalist and a more sociable person, order "How to Be Popular." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Popularity Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Illinois arts groups lined up for federal ‘America 250’ money. What version of our history will they tell?
entries.summary: <p>America celebrates its 250th birthday on July 4. Local audiences can expect a wave of patriotic programming in spring and summer, from a live reading of the Declaration of Independence at the Newberry Library to a staging of the Tony-winning musical “1776” by DLO Musical Theatre in Danville.</p><p>As part of a change in its guidelines, the National Endowment for the Arts shifted its focus last year to funding initiatives that celebrate the milestone. About 20 Illinois cultural groups received money through the NEA’s “America 250” emphasis to total $661,000 — about half of the state’s total $1.2 million haul.</p><div class="Enhancement review of materials at Smithsonian museums after deeming some displays on race, slavery and queer history “divisive.” The federal funding windfall has forced cultural groups to reckon with a tough question: How far can you go to interrogate the full and complex history of the country while also celebrating its progress?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div Harris is the editor of “Obsidian: Literature & Arts in the African Diaspora.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Dawn M. Joseph</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Leaders of Illinois organizations say they are committed to taking a deep and nuanced look at the nation while also amplifying diverse perspectives. Among them is Duriel E. Harris, editor of “<a class="Link" href="https://obsidianlit.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Obsidian: Literature & Arts in the African Diaspora</u></a>,” a literary journal published by Illinois State University. In the forthcoming issue “Bond of a Nation: Ekphrasis & the Promise of America,” writers will examine the values of democracy.</p><p>“We have recognized for some time that [the country] is not living up to those ideals,” Harris said. “The essence of love is truth-telling. How do we evolve and become the best that we can be? It is through constructive criticism.”</p><h2>National Endowment for the Arts stabilizes following 2025 cuts</h2><p>The NEA-supported programming for “America 250” spans institutions of all sizes and audiences of all ages and comes at a time when the Trump administration is limiting history education by <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/ending-radical-indoctrination-in-k-12-schooling/" target="_blank" ><u>banning “discriminatory equity ideology”</u></a> in schools and <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/10/national-monuments-trump-rewrite-history-racism-indigenous-people" target="_blank" ><u>removing references to slavery and racism</u></a> at national parks and historic sites.</p><p>The government has also pushed back on criticism of certain historical figures by erecting a <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-christopher-columbus-statue-4138894aaecd9c2ad5e9cc2b474137ab" target="_blank" ><u>statue of Christopher Columbus</u></a> near the White House, and reinstalling a previously toppled <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2025/10/27/nx-s1-5587824/confederate-statue-albert-pike-trump" target="_blank" ><u>statue of Confederate general Albert Pike</u></a> in Judiciary Park.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e20000" name="image-e20000"></a> One exhibit on political illustrations will feature both patriotic and dissenting commentary about the country during times of war.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At the Newberry Library, the aim is to offer “thought-provoking exhibitions,” said Jill Austin, who serves as the organization’s vice president of public engagement. “Free and Independent” will break down the “problems and the promise” of the Declaration of Independence, while highlighting the perspectives of tribal nations and those who took issue with slavery at the time, according to Austin.</p><p>An exhibit on <a class="Link" href="https://www.newberry.org/calendar/conceived-in-liberty" target="_blank" ><u>political illustrations</u></a> will feature both patriotic and dissenting commentary about the country during times of war. Other relevant programming includes a presentation of Revolution-era items during “Newberry Fest” and the NEA-supported “Impressive Textiles: Printing on Fabric,” which includes a commemorative handkerchief celebrating George Washington and campaign banners for Abraham Lincoln.</p><p>“We wanted to go beyond a celebration to really explore the history and help people understand the nuances of it more deeply through our collections,” Austin said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s “America 250" programming will incorporate cultural diversity through a staging of “Lincoln Portrait & Ellington Harlem,” featuring Duke Ellington’s love letter to the iconic Black neighborhood and Aaron Copland’s tribute to Abraham Lincoln. The latter will be narrated by Chicago actor Harry Lennix. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>With a lineup that includes works by George Gershwin, Charles Ives and John Williams, the Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s “America 250” programming primarily reflects traditionally celebrated American classical music. But the CSO will also add some cultural diversity through its staging of “Lincoln Portrait & Ellington Harlem,” featuring Duke Ellington’s love letter to the iconic Black neighborhood and Aaron Copland’s tribute to Abraham Lincoln. </p><p>The latter will be narrated by Harry Lennix, a Chicago actor known for his advocacy for Black artists. Performances of “Liberty” (Symphony No. 5)” by Wynton Marsalis’ Jazz at Lincoln Center Orchestra and spirituals by the Apostolic Church of God Sanctuary Choir also contribute to the lineup of diverse voices.</p><p>“Everybody has a role to play in the history of this country,” said Cristina Rocca, CSO’s vice president of artistic administration. “Diversity should always be celebrated. I hope that people will see the importance of this celebration and to get to know more about American music, which is so rich in its breadth and variety of styles.”</p><p>For Claire Rice, of Arts Alliance Illinois, the state’s NEA programming will offer an inclusive view of American history.</p><p>“The ways in which folks are interpreting that 250 mandate are demonstrative of the richness and diversity of the cultural fabric of the state,” said Rice, who is the executive director of the arts advocacy organization. “Illinois was never going to be a state where it was only one kind of grant for America 250.”</p><p>But the NEA grants have come with controversy. Last year, the federal arts funding group <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2025/05/06/nea-grants-trump-chicago-cultural-events" target="_blank" ><u>clawed back grants</u></a> promised to arts organizations across the country amid an ideological shift. It <a class="Link" href="https://www.arts.gov/grants/legal-requirements-and-assurance-of-compliance/frequently-asked-questions" target="_blank" ><u>changed its guidelines</u></a>, requiring grantseekers to certify that they do not operate diversity, equity and inclusion programs that violate federal anti-discrimination laws, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2025/02/14/trumps-new-arts-funding-guidelines-chicago-nea-arts-grants-jeopardy" target="_blank" ><u>eliminating “Challenge America” grants</u></a> that went to smaller arts groups that focus on underserved communities.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure The walking tour is highlighted on a downloadable passport as part of the Illinois America 250 initiative. The walking tour is highlighted on a downloadable passport as part of the Illinois America 250 initiative. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The shift in federal funding impacted organizations’ ability to do everything from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-culture/2025/09/24/chicago-free-festivals-national-endowment-for-the-arts-funding-nea-grants" target="_blank" ><u>offering free programming</u></a> to touring their shows.</p><p>“There are pockets of the country in which no private funding is going to support the arts,” said Erin Harkey, executive director of Americans for the Arts, a national advocacy organization. “Public funding is really about access and participation. So a loss in those funds is really significant.”</p><p>Trump’s attempt to eliminate the NEA budget altogether was thwarted by a bipartisan vote from Congress, which funded the agency for $207 million for 2026. After a rocky period, advocates said the recent confirmation of NEA Chair Mary Anne Carter, who served in the role during Trump’s first administration, has stabilized the agency.</p><p>Organizations should be encouraged to keep applying, Harkey said.</p><p>“Continuing to engage with the National Endowment for the Arts and not making assumptions about what will be funded and what will not is an important thing to do,” Harkey said.</p><h2>Ballads for Trump’s National Garden of American Heroes</h2><p>Changes in federal guidelines have forced organizations to adapt their programming. Last year, the Sones de Mexico Ensemble sought NEA funding to support performances of corridos, or traditional Mexican ballads that often present heroic tales. </p><p>After being denied twice, the Mexican folk music group and educational organization altered its programming to pitch corridos about eight figures in Trump’s forthcoming National Garden of American Heroes: Muhammad Ali, Roberto Clemente, Dorothy Day, Joseph H. De Castro, Amelia Earhart, Mary Fields, Woody Guthrie and Abraham Lincoln. The grant was approved.</p><p>The ensemble has plans to perform the corridos at the Chicago History Museum in June.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5e322c9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F45%2F18%2F280b877f4caa95cb8ea460587e6d%2Famerica250-260331-008.jpg
