class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“I’m trying to roll with the punches,” Juan Dies, co-founder of the Sones de Mexico Ensemble, said. “I’m trying to adapt. My organization is not political. We have an artistic mission to promote greater appreciation of Mexican music and culture, and I feel like we can carry that on under these guidelines.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella for the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I wanted to have some Latino representation,” said Juan Dies, executive director of the Sones de Mexico Ensemble. “I wanted to have some men and women represented. I wanted to have African Americans represented. And I wanted to show the versatility of corridos. Why not have these songs accepted?”</p><p>Dies said he supports NEA staffers and will continue to apply for funding.</p><p>“I'm trying to roll with the punches,” he said. “I'm trying to adapt. My organization is not political. We have an artistic mission to promote greater appreciation of Mexican music and culture, and I feel like we can carry that on under these guidelines.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b30000" name="image-b30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/598e592/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aa3d223/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8089066/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Juan Díes, co-founder of the Sones de Mexico Ensemble, teaches guitar at the Arturo Velasquez Westside Technical Institute, Saturday, March 21, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8089066/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27c7a12/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8089066/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6591x3699+0+348/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F25%2Fb586001b4c4e9e73196251e50623%2Famerica250-260331-010.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Juan Dies, co-founder of the Sones de Mexico Ensemble, teaches guitar at the Arturo Velasquez Westside Technical Institute.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella for the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Likewise, the Illinois America 250 initiative — which did not receive state or federal funding — aims to similarly uplift the contributions of different cultures, with special emphasis on African Americans, indigenous communities and immigrants. A <a class="Link" href="https://www.il250.org/passport" target="_blank" ><u>downloadable passport</u></a> encourages tourism to such diverse landmarks as the Black Hawk State Historic Site in Rock Island, the National Museum of Mexican Art in Pilsen, and the Owen Lovejoy Homestead, which is an Underground Railroad site in Princeton.</p><p>The passport also educates visitors about the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-history/2025/11/08/chicago-race-riot-1919-memorial-loop" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Race Riot of 1919 Commemoration Project</u></a>. Markers throughout the city memorialize Black and white residents killed during attacks by white mobs during the violent week. The project was co-founded by Peter Cole, a history professor at Western Illinois University.</p><p>Reflecting on America's 250th anniversary, Cole said he believes in celebrating both the aspirations of the Declaration of Independence, while also acknowledging the darker parts of the nation’s history.</p><p>“It's not to rub everybody's face in the fact that America is terrible,” he said. “It’s actually evidence that America has gotten better over time. Most Americans are smart enough to be able to distinguish between horrible things of the past, and that we shouldn't have done them, but that we are not the same people now as we might have been then.”<br></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: In Illinois, a diverse lineup celebrates America’s 250th anniversary. Here are some highlights.
entries.summary: <p>The Prairie State has a lot planned for the nation’s semiquincentennial this year. From exhibits and plays to concerts and panels, cultural organizations are offering a slew of celebratory and thought-provoking programs. Read on for just a fraction of the highlights.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.newberry.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Newberry Library’s</u></b></a><b> </b>commemorative programming runs throughout the spring and summer, with an exhibit on the Declaration of Independence and a live reading of the historic document opening April 9. Other highlights include an exhibit on wartime political illustrations and a Newberry Fest event featuring Revolution-era items. April 9 - Sept. 19. 60 W. Walton St. Admission: Free.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-350000" name="image-350000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4943d0c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ecc1809/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11c062e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Newberry Library located at 60 W. Walton St., Tuesday, March 24, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11c062e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7faf7b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11c062e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F55%2Fa0daa54143d8b712cf28a8256c09%2Famerica250-260402-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Newberry Library’s commemorative programming runs throughout the spring and summer.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Influential American artists will be honored with 10-foot sculptures as part of “Walking with Giants,” an outdoor exhibition presented by the <a class="Link" href="https://www.theccma.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Cleve Carney Museum of Art</u></b></a>, McAninch Art Center and College of DuPage Foundation. Chicago’s Judith Mayer and Naperville’s Rich Lo will design the works, which will depict Dolly Parton, Lin-Manuel Miranda, Jean-Michel Basquiat and more. June through the summer. McAninch Art Center, 425 Fawell Blvd., Glen Ellyn. Admission: Free.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://cso.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Chicago Symphony Orchestra</u></b></a> will stage a series of concerts all June saluting American composers, including George Gershwin, Charles Ives, John Williams and Wynton Marsalis. One of the standout shows, “Lincoln Portrait & Ellington Harlem,” will feature classic pieces by Duke Ellington and Aaron Copland, with narration by Chicago actor Harry Lennix. June 2-27. Symphony Center, 220 S. Michigan Ave. Admission: Varies.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagobotanic.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Chicago Botanic Garden</u></b></a> will explore the history of gardening in the U.S. with its series, “America Grows: 250 Years of Garden Stories.” The organization will offer themed panels, exhibitions and walking tours. Visitors can view the first-known American cookbook and see crops grown in victory and post-emancipation gardens. June 5 - Sept. 30. Chicago Botanic Garden, 1000 Lake Cook Road. Free with admission.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.sifest.com/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Southern Illinois Music Festival</u></b></a> is offering more than two weeks of celebratory events, including five orchestral programs and five Patriotic Pops concerts featuring three centuries of American music. June 18 - July 4. Various locations. Admission: Varies.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.dlomusicaltheatre.com/" target="_blank" ><b><u>DLO Musical Theatre</u></b></a> will stage a production of “1776,” the Tony-winning musical by Book by Peter Stone and Sherman Edwards. Co-produced by the Scottish Rite – Valley of Danville, the play follows the founding fathers’ quest to break free from the British monarchy. June 26-28. Danville Masonic Temple, 109 W. North St., Danville. Admission: $23.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-040000" name="image-040000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aac314d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/defd418/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/edb02c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Juan Díes, co-founder of the Sones de Mexico Ensemble, poses for a portrait on Saturday, March 21, 2026 outside the Arturo Velasquez Westside Technical Institute, where he teaches guitar. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/edb02c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e527670/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/edb02c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F81%2F7f5a8b404952b635a94464e82d63%2Famerica250-260331-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Audiences will hear heroic ballads in English and Spanish inspired by Muhammad Ali, Roberto Clemente, Dorothy Day and more from the Sones de Mexico Ensemble.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella for the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://sonesdemexico.com/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Sones de Mexico Ensemble</u></b></a> will perform corridos, or traditional Mexican ballads, inspired by heroes in President Donald Trump’s forthcoming National Garden of American Heroes. Audiences will hear heroic tales in English and Spanish about some of the following figures: Muhammad Ali, Roberto Clemente, Dorothy Day, Joseph H. De Castro, Amelia Earhart, Mary Fields, Woody Guthrie and Abraham Lincoln. June 27, 1 p.m. Chicago History Museum, 1601 N. Clark St. Admission: Free.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagohistory.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Chicago History Museum</u></b></a> is honoring America’s 250th birthday by presenting rotating art installations in the lobby and making updates to its “Facing Freedom in America” exhibition, which reopens July 4. Other attractions include a forthcoming historical clothing exhibition. Programming runs throughout the year. Chicago History Museum, 601 N. Clark St. Admission: $19.</p><p>As part of the city’s official “America 250” celebrations, the<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/dca/supp_info/air_water_show.html" target="_blank" ><b><u>Chicago Air and Water Show</u></b></a> will incorporate the country’s semiquincentennial into its weekend of events. Chicago’s cultural contributions to the U.S. will be highlighted during the lakeside spectacle. Aug. 15-16, 10:30 a.m. to 3 p.m. Lakefront between Fullerton Avenue and Oak Street. Admission: Free.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.architecture.org/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Chicago Architecture Cente</u></b><u>r</u></a> will highlight the city’s role as a “manufacturing and railroad hub” during Open House Chicago, which showcases notable architectural sites through public tours. October. Various locations. Admission: Free.</p><p><i>This list will be updated.</i></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: Obama Presidential Center to host summer basketball, volleyball programs for South Side teens
entries.summary: <p>The Obama Presidential Center will kick off its youth programming this summer with opportunities for South Side teens to play volleyball and basketball and build leadership skills.</p><p>The coed programs are part of an initiative announced Thursday between the Obama Foundation and the nonprofit After School Matters, which will serve as the center’s main youth programming partner <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/07/obama-presidential-center-opening-juneteenth-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>when it opens on June 19</u></a>. They are the first of what will be an expanding catalog of programs for teens at the center.</p><p>The Obama Foundation also plans to partner with other organizations to offer youth programming, including My Brother’s Keeper Alliance, Girls Opportunity Alliance and Chicago Public Schools, though the details of those collaborations are still being worked out. There’s plans for youth programming around arts and media, city gardening, cooking and more.</p><p>The two sports programs will run from July to August and are open to Chicago high schoolers ages 14 to 18 who live in or go to school in the South Shore, Woodlawn or Washington Park communities. Like other After School Matters offerings, teens will earn a stipend for participating and completing the programs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" </div><p>Interested teens should <a class="Link" href="https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSd9sFJifilmtohFf-tbmGEefph0ZPf-F-feK2vh47nabKB7ww/viewform" target="_blank" ><u>submit an online form</u></a> by April 15 to be considered for a spot. It’s expected about 100 teens will participate in the first wave of youth programs at the center.</p><p>Joe Kennedy, director of athletics and sports leadership at the Obama Presidential Center, said it was important to start with those neighborhoods because they’ve been involved in getting the campus off the ground.</p><p>“These communities have been part of this journey with us,” Kennedy said. “We want to be intentional about some of our initial opportunities that we get a chance to bring these moments, programs, resources into the communities.”</p><p>The youth programs will be held at the Home Court facility on former President Barack Obama’s namesake museum and campus. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2025/12/08/obama-presidential-center-home-court-building-opened-exclusive-look" target="_blank" ><u>The 60,000-square-foot facility includes</u></a> a professional-sized basketball court, community rooms and an exercise area.</p><p>The programs are meant to encourage teens to be active while instilling leadership, teamwork and other skills young people can use throughout their lives. Program instructors will also focus on teens’ mental health.</p><p>The Obamas “believe deeply in the power of sport to be a way to transform lives and build civic habits and strengthen communities,” Kennedy said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> and gardening.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Christopher Dilts/The Obama Foundation</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mary Ellen Caron, CEO of After School Matters, said the organization already has programs in those South Side neighborhoods and this partnership will add more opportunities for teens. The nonprofit and the foundation are providing the space and staff for these two initial programs and are planning to fundraise together in the future as the partnership expands.</p><p>“We’re making sure that those young people know that the Obama Center is there and that it’s going to be an accessible place for teenagers,” Caron said.</p><p>Caron also hopes to offer programs that introduce teens to the trades, something young people frequently ask about. The organization has around 50 trades programs across the city.</p><p>“It’s vitally important that we continue to look at teens and make sure to find out what they like, what they don’t like, really care about them, so that they are engaged,” Caron said.</p><p>The partnership with the Obama Presidential Center marks the latest expansion of After School Matter’s offerings. <a class="Link" href="https://afterschoolmatters.org/after-school-matters-opens-new-teen-center-in-cabrini-community/" target="_blank" ><u>Last year, the nonprofit opened a new facility</u></a> near the former Cabrini-Green public housing site on the Near North Side that includes a theater, gym, art studios and science and technology labs.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: No tanks: Bulls' Tre Jones knows all about risk and reward of tanking
entries.summary: <p>Tre Jones understands the power of losing games and tanking.</p><p>He was a third-year guard for San Antonio in that 2022-23 campaign, mired in losing and disappointment throughout that season with the specter of a 7-foot-4 French kid looming over the entire bottom half of the league.</p><p>The Spurs went into that lottery rolling a 14% chance to hit gold and did just that, ending up with No. 1 and Victor Wembanyama.</p><p>A prize that had Jones still smiling when he recalled it this week. The journey to get there, however? Still painful.</p><p>“Everyone was excited for sure after the lottery,” Jones said. “We knew (Wembanyama) was a generational talent coming out. You knew about the stories of his workouts and things like that. But to go in and see the work back up the talent once we drafted him, which is what you love to hear, so we were definitely excited about that.</p><p>“Obviously, what we weren’t excited about during the season was winning only 22 games, losing however many we did in a row that year (16). It wears on you.”</p><p>So while Jones’ current Bulls team isn’t as bad as a few of those Spurs seasons, 29 wins and losing every game in the month of February doesn’t exactly build a team up with confidence.</p><p>But that doesn’t mean Jones is naïve to what’s been going on since the trade deadline. Albeit too late in the season, the Bulls front office made the decision to weaken the roster and increase the lottery odds, hoping to take advantage of a very talented 2026 draft class.</p><p>While there is no Wemby at the top, there is star power in the top four, and potentially all the way down to where the Bulls currently sit before the lottery shakeup – at No. 9.</p><p>That’s the chase for the front office and fan base to embrace. The coaches and players? No thanks.</p><p>“There were frustrations that went on (in San Antonio) during that season that weren’t good, so a season like that during the year is very frustrating for us as players because that’s our livelihood, that’s what we get paid to do,” Jones said. “It should be frustrating losing games, but obviously that summer you receive the No. 1 pick and you know who that No. 1 pick is. You know it’s someone that will change the franchise around, potentially.</p><p>“But even when we drafted him Year 1, it’s not like we came out and were back. We had another top five pick the following year after that (Stephon Castle No. 4). So yes, you are excited when you get that No. 1 pick because you do know you’re getting that guy that has a chance to change the franchise, but the journey to get there isn’t easy on you.”</p><p>With just six games left, the Bulls have a one-game edge in the lottery over Milwaukee, and it was a good sign that they understood the assignment against lowly Indiana on Wednesday, sitting Josh Giddey with left hamstring tightness.</p><p>However, they still have the third easiest schedule remaining, with two games in Washington and a season finale in Dallas.</p><p>What’s at stake is a 7% difference between No. 9 and No. 10 in hitting lottery top four.</p><p>Just don’t try selling Jones on that.</p><p>“I’m just a firm believer in I was paid to come here to help this team win and I think every single night I can do that for my team,” Jones said. “There are things going around as far as teams tanking, and it’s something I’ve been a part of in the past. Obviously, there are thoughts about that, but as far as showing up to a game and competing, every game I’m in I feel like I can win, and that’s it.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: After a prominent dance venue closed, a new effort aims to lift up independent Chicago dancemakers
entries.summary: <p>A year after the storied Chicago dance venue Links Hall <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/dance/2025/04/17/chicago-links-hall-dance-closing-small-venue-financial-trouble-arts" target="_blank" ><u>announced it would close</u></a> its doors for good, prompting an outcry from the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/dance/2025/06/26/links-hall-closing-chicago-dance" target="_blank" ><u>experimental dance</u></a> community that staged works there, a new effort seeks to help fill the gap.</p><p>The nonprofit Chicago Dancemakers Forum announced Thursday the inaugural class of its Dance Project Grants Program, which will help power 10 live dance events in the next year from independent artists. Events range from a weeklong footwork series to a dance theater performance featuring puppets and projections.</p><p>“I'm not going to let the void just be the void,” said Joanna Furnans, the forum’s executive director. “I feel very firmly that we need to do something else.”</p><p>Each of the 10 recipients, selected from a pool of more than 100 applicants, will receive $10,000 in funding, which comes from foundations, the state arts council and individuals. On the list of grantees are dancemaker and poet J'Sun Howard and the Afro-feminist-focused group, Honey Pot Performance.</p><p>The program arrives amid a precarious moment for the arts. Last year, President Donald Trump <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2025/05/06/nea-grants-trump-chicago-cultural-events" target="_blank" ><u>clawed back grant dollars</u></a> awarded through the National Endowment for the Arts and shifted the program’s focus, in part, to projects that celebrate the nation’s 250th birthday. (The Dancemakers Forum was among the arts organizations that lost federal grant dollars.) Locally, funding for the arts has <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/city-hall/2025/11/05/chicago-dcase-cultural-affairs-budget-clinee-hedspeth-kenya-merritt-grants-arts" target="_blank" ><u>dipped at the city level</u></a> and remained <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/02/23/pritzker-budget-arts-funding-flat-illinois-2026-2027-grants-trump" target="_blank" ><u>stagnant in the proposed state budget</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-780000" name="image-780000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9490a78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+53/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2F63%2F6f7b49e24dfe8195fbb4eef55fc9%2Fswisher-by-jsun-howard.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“SWIS)HER” by dancemaker and poet J’Sun Howard is among the grantees. The work is a celebration of Black queer dance lineages. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The shaky funding landscape has led <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/mar/01/art-museum-of-americas-exhibit-trump-dei" target="_blank" ><u>some arts organizations</u></a> to <a class="Link" href="https://gothamist.com/news/some-nyc-artists-are-avoiding-work-about-race-and-gender-amid-federal-arts-funding-cuts" target="_blank" ><u>shy away from works that</u></a> embrace diversity or subject matters that may attract scrutiny. Furnans hopes the forum’s grant program can be an antidote to that.</p><p>Chicago is home to a few major dance companies that tour internationally, plus a handful of midsize professional groups. But the scene has also produced countless independent dancemakers who need rehearsal and performance space to stage their works; that scene has been hit hardest by the Links’ closure. “I think historically, Chicago has a conservative dance mind, or dance aesthetic. By Chicago, I mean the major stages are showing work that feels kind of like safe and, you know, tried and true,” she said. “There are so many other kinds of work being made in the city that we are in a position to uplift.”</p><p>Furnans acknowledged that while “$10,000 is great,” it also is “not enough for any of these projects.” But, she’s hoping the investment will generate momentum and help rebuild a pathway for individual dance artists to establish careers in Chicago.</p><p>“We're starting to lose the kind of stepping stone opportunities for professional dancemakers to build their careers and have continuous opportunities to make a name for themselves in the city and beyond,” Furnans said.</p><p>For years, the dance nonprofit gave four larger grants to mid-career artists through its Lab Artists Program, but after surveying the local landscape, Furnans opted to pivot and put that initiative on pause in favor of smaller grants for dancemakers still establishing themselves.</p><p>“I was seeing that the scaffold has left, and so we need to come in with the resources that we have and actually do something different to help stimulate the ecosystem at this time,” Furnans said.</p><p>While each of the grantees’ projects will result in a live performance or event, the grant does not provide a set venue or rehearsal space. The hope is to infuse dance into new and unexpected spaces, Furnans said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-970000" name="image-970000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83236e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d7dd3c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/926cd84/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="La Cookout by Amanda Ramirez.png" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/926cd84/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26b018b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/926cd84/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+76/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F52%2F336185084f74855fe0875ebc06e9%2Fla-cookout-by-amanda-ramirez.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Amanda Ramirez’s work, “La Cookout,” was inspired by her upbringing in El Paso, Texas. She first began developing the site-specific immersive dance performance during a residency at the Chicago Cultural Center. Now, she says this new grant funding will allow her to finish the work. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Taking art beyond the confines of theaters is something <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2026/02/05/david-byrne-chicago-theater-of-the-mind-goodman-talking-heads" target="_blank" ><u>larger organizations</u></a> are also experimenting with as they vie to attract new audiences, but dance artists like Amanda Ramirez say permanence also matters.</p><p>“Links Hall was very important to me as an artist and its loss is massive,” said Ramirez, who also pointed to Hamlin Park Theater and Chicago Moving Company’s closure in 2024. “I personally have felt a massive decrease in performances happening. There's definitely still performances, and people are resilient, but to me, there is a ginormous hole in the community.”</p><p>Ramirez, a 35-year-old dancemaker who lives in Chicago’s Logan Square, is among the grantees. Her project, “La Cookout,” is a site-specific immersive dance performance, inspired by family cookouts during her upbringing in El Paso, Texas.</p><p>“It brings together dance, music and food to create a sort of Latin-style cookout, or, how we call it, a carne asada, and it becomes a living and participatory performance experience,” said Ramirez, who first began the project through a residency at the Chicago Cultural Center. “This grant gives me the opportunity to finish the project so that it can be performed in a non-traditional environment.”</p><p>In addition to the grant program, which Furnans said will also be offered next year, the Dancemakers Forum has partnered with High Concept Labs to take the pulse of the city’s experimental performance sector. Following a <a class="Link" href="https://chicagodancemakers.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/11/HCL-CDF-Chicago-Performance-Community-Survey-2025-Report-1.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>survey last fall</u></a>, they’ll co-host a <a class="Link" href="https://www.meetdaisy.co/calendars/high-concept-labs/events/chicago-performance-community-gathering" target="_blank" ><u>community gathering</u></a> on April 18 to take the temperature of the local scene and discuss what comes next.</p><h3><b>2026 Dance Project Grants Recipients: </b></h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-bb1fed02-2e02-11f1-b9fb-63040e01ef04"><li>“La Cookout” by Amanda Ramirez</li><li>“TZINELAS” by Anniela Huidobro Castro</li><li>“little fears” by Ashwaty Chennat</li><li>“MATADORA” by Camila Rivero Pooley aka INSÉKTA</li><li>“SWIS)HER” by J'Sun Howard</li><li>“Untitled (Elephant)” by Lin Hixson and Every house has a door</li><li>“Swine Ball” by Nora Sharp</li><li>“Pleasure Power Portal” by Meida McNeal and Honey Pot Performance</li><li>“Chicago Footworkology Week” by Mike D Chicago and Creative Netwerk</li><li>“Untitled” by Zachary Nicol with Anna Martine Whitehead and Tara Aisha Willis</li></ul><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ.</i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Person fatally struck by Metra train near Geneva station causing extensive delays
entries.summary: <p>A person was fatally struck by a Metra train causing extensive delays in the west suburbs Thursday morning. </p><p>Inbound Union Pacific West train #18 hit the person near the Geneva station, 328 Crescent Pl., around 6 a.m., according to Metra officials. </p><p>The person was pronounced dead at the scene, officials said. Their age or gender wasn’t immediately known. </p><p>Inbound and outbound trains were halted until 7:30 a.m. and delays were between 15 minutes and an hour and a half, according to Metra. </p><p>No further information was immediately available. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 'Springlike storms' bring threat of gusty winds, hail, flash flooding and warmer temps to Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>Rounds of "spring-like storms" could potentially bring winds up to 70 mph, 70-degree temperatures, hail, flash flooding and an isolated tornado to the Chicago area Thursday. </p><p>While some rainfall has already reached the area Thursday morning, the best chance for severe weather will be from about 3 p.m. to 9 p.m., according to the National Weather Service. The temperature at O'Hare was about 40 degrees as of 8 a.m. with highs expected to climb to nearly 70 degrees later Thursday. </p><p>The most intense storms could produce winds up to 70 mph, quarter-sized hail and the possibility of a tornado, the weather service said. Northwest Illinois is most at risk for a tornado, though one touching down is possible anywhere in the Chicago area, officials said. </p><p>A wind advisory was in effect for the Chicago area from 1 p.m. to 7 p.m., according to the weather service. </p><p>"It's a lot of little things going on here today, Zachary Yack, a meteorologist at the weather service, said. "We just advise people to keep an eye on the forecast here today, and of course if one is issued for your area, seek proper precaution."</p><p>The storm system behind the potentially severe weather was a "typical springtime storm system" moving through Kansas City into Iowa, Yack said. The Chicago area was on the "warm" side of the system which is driving the showers and storms. </p><p>The threat for severe storms will linger into Friday, though it's uncertain whether those storms will reach the Chicago area due to the uncertainties of the front's placement and timing, according to the weather service. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

La Shawn K. Ford holds his head as Resilience Healthcare CEO Manoj Prasad explains West Suburban Medical Center’s finances Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-d/" target="_blank" >West Suburban Medical Center struggled to treat patients months before closing</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-h/" target="_blank" >Kristen Schorsch</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-k/" target="_blank" >Kaitlin Washburn</a></h5><p><b>Bold promises</b>: Dr. Manoj Prasad, owner of West Suburban Medical Center, pledged to do everything he could to reopen the safety net hospital by this summer as he defended his leadership in a wide-ranging, combative and chaotic news conference Wednesday. State Rep. La Shawn Ford, whose district includes West Suburban, organized the press conference with Prasad, and said he was there as a representative for the community.</p><p><b>Our findings</b>: Hospital inspection reports obtained by WBEZ and the Sun-Times paint a picture of broader problems at the Oak Park facility, one that shows a hospital struggling to take care of its most critically ill patients — and even keep the heat on — in the months before West Suburban closed.</p><p><b>Health care headache</b>: Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-u/" target="_blank" >temporarily suspended services at </a>West Suburban last week, saying he had no choice because the hospital’s electronic medical record system was not properly billing for services. But in February and March, the hospital was investigated by the federal government twice for "immediate jeopardy," a serious allegation meaning inspectors believed patients could be at risk of serious harm or death.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Patients, lawmakers and community leaders have criticized how abruptly West Suburban closed March 25. The for-profit hospital mostly treats low-income and elderly patients. The majority are Black or Hispanic. But there were signs that West Suburban was unraveling. Prasad had <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-o/" target="_blank" >closed his other hospital</a>, Weiss Memorial in Uptown, in August.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-b/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>IMMIGRATION ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div target="_blank" >Immigrant leaders are anxious as Supreme Court hears arguments on birthright citizenship</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-p/" target="_blank" >Mark Sherman</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-x/" target="_blank" >Adriana Cardona-Maguigad</a></h5><p><b>Major case</b>: The U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday took up one of the term’s most consequential cases, President Donald Trump’s executive order on birthright citizenship declaring that children born to parents who are in the U.S. illegally or temporarily are not American citizens. A definitive ruling is expected by early summer.</p><p><b>Community on edge</b>: Immigrant leaders in Chicago say they're anxious about the case and what the outcome could mean for families across the country.  Grace Chan McKibben, executive director of the Coalition for a Better Chinese American Community, said many communities are worried that some children could lose their legal status or end up with no legal status at all.</p><p><b>In the hot seat</b>: Solicitor General D. John Sauer argued the government's case, facing one skeptical after another. The justices asked about the legal basis for the order and voiced more practical concerns.</p><p><b>More on immigration</b>:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-18ab7d20-2e96-11f1-8f72-a3bdc584593f"><li>A DePaul University student <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-m/" target="_blank" >was charged with spitting on a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent working at O’Hare Airport</a>. His lawyer said it may have been unintentional.</li><li>Joabe Barbosa, a Brazilian runner who has jogged on nearly every street in Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-q/" target="_blank" >is facing departure as his student visa expires</a>.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" Chicago Police Supt. Larry Snelling said the proposed changes would allow for better supervision, greater opportunities for community policing and more consistent response times across neighborhoods.</p><p><b>Labor lens</b>: The study, commissioned by the City Council two years ago, also recommends shifting 600 officers to the streets from jobs that can be filled for less money by civilians. But the department’s collective bargaining agreements with police unions could limit its ability to make any sweeping changes.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-e/" Local audiences can expect a wave of patriotic programming in spring and summer, from a live reading of the Declaration of Independence at the Newberry Library to a staging of the musical "1776" by DLO Musical Theatre in Danville.</p><p><b>Federal funds</b>: As part of a change in its guidelines, the National Endowment for the Arts shifted its focus last year to funding initiatives that celebrate the milestone. About 20 Illinois cultural groups received money through the NEA’s "America 250" emphasis totaling $661,000 — about half of the state’s $1.2 million haul.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: In his second term, President Trump has focused on U.S. history and culture, including instituting an unprecedented review of materials at Smithsonian museums after deeming some displays on race, slavery and queer history "divisive." The federal funding has forced cultural groups to reckon with a tough question: How far can you go to interrogate the full and complex history of the country while also celebrating its progress?</p><p><b>Local programming</b>: The city and state are rolling out their own slate of programs, too: Illinois America 250 and the Chicago America 250 initiatives will feature public art installations, exhibits, music festivals, educational tours and more — <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-yk/" target="_blank" >here are some highlights</a>.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-yu/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>How would you like to see Chicago and Illinois commemorate America's 250th birthday?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Amy Lendian is acting systems engineer manager for the Artemis II lunar orbital mission and was the fire suppression and console engineer for the Artemis I rocket.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Chicagoan helps launch Artemis II, NASA’s first moon mission in 53 years</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-ju/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p>Amy Lendian, a Chicago resident, remembered watching Apollo 8 lift off in 1968 as she shook off nerves heading to work Wednesday morning.</p><p>She was set to enter the control room at the Kennedy Space Center for what could have been a 16-hour shift. Her mind was on the mission from 58 years ago, which helped turn her dreams toward the stars.</p><p>Now 67 and the acting systems engineer manager for the Artemis II lunar orbital mission, who had been the fire suppression and console engineer for the Artemis I rocket, she was thinking back on that 9-year-old girl who watched "almost the same" mission in which she would play a part Wednesday.</p><p>"As a kid I wanted to be an astronaut," Lendian said. “ So for me this has a very special meaning."</p><p>Lendian was called out of retirement to helm a team of four engineers in the control room, monitoring systems that range from water and electrical to fire suppression in case of an emergency — a much larger task with an actual crew onboard. Artemis I was unmanned.</p><p>Artemis II is the opening shot of NASA’s grand plans for a permanent moon base. Three Americans and one Canadian are set to fly around the moon without stopping or even orbiting — then head straight back for a Pacific splashdown. The rocket took off shortly after 5:30 p.m. without issue, starting the astronauts' 10-day journey.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-tl/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-ty/" target="_blank" >What’s the best burger you’ve had in Chicago?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said, edited for length and clarity...</p><p>"My favorite burger in the city is the Bacon Jam Smashburger from Lucy’s in Uptown. The bacon jam and garlic aioli combo is mouthwatering!" — <i>Kaitlin Janis</i></p><p>"Top Notch Beefburgers in Beverly. No fancy toppings. Just delicious tasting burgers ground in-house, and the perfect bun. I love it with the sharp American cheese. Great fries and shakes. It’s been my favorite for decades." — <i>Edward Zelnis</i></p><p>"Hands down (actually two hands down then up to your mouth) is Monk’s Pub's Bacon Swiss Burger with a slice of raw onion and a tomato ... Been going there for 40 years. The other places listed in your article may be good but they are too expensive and fancy. Give me old school every time." — <i>Stan Kaderbek</i></p><p>"Gretel. I've never seen a burger with foie gras and egg before. It's a $50 burger and it's the best burger I've ever had." — <i>K. Tecun</i></p><p>I had a burger at BurgerBelly years ago that was done in conjunction with Gale Street Inn. It was called the Leadbelly Burgers Rib'n Bleus Burger. It contained [sriracha] bleu cheese slaw, a giant patty made of fresh beef ground with smoked pork belly, a quarter-slab of baby back ribs ... and a pile of fried onion straws, all on their house made bun. It was a full seven inches tall and I honestly don't know how I got my mouth around it, but I did and it was spectacular!" — <i>Michael Oelrich</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-190000" name="image-190000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52b8e83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04eee81/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a023007/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The crowd at the Adler Planetarium awaits the launch on April 1, 2026 of the Artemis II rocket, carrying a crew of three on NASA's first moon mission in more than five decades. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a023007/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/05e37e0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a023007/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5371x3014+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F13%2F42%2F646d48d34e9daacfe71be299dfd1%2Fartemisiilaunch-260402-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A crowd <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wilkhkd-l-tt/" target="_blank" >watches the Artemis II launch at the Adler Planetarium</a> on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. entries.title: Trump says Pam Bondi is out as his attorney general
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump said Thursday that <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pam-bondi-justice-department-trump-attorney-general-4b94c094cfcabf606e4883fe709ab55a" target="_blank" >Pam Bondi</a> is out as his attorney general, ending the contentious tenure of a loyalist who upended the Justice Department’s culture of independence from the White House, oversaw large-scale firings of career employees and moved aggressively to investigate the Republican president’s perceived enemies.</p><p>The announcement follows months of scrutiny over the Justice Department’s handling of <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pam-bondi-house-judiciary-committee-justice-department-6d7502b80e42e9e9454264e242507bbd" target="_blank" >files related to Jeffrey Epstein’s sex trafficking investigation</a> that made Bondi the target of angry conservatives even with her close relationship with Trump. She also struggled to satisfy Trump’s demands to prosecute his political rivals, with multiple investigations rejected by judges or grand juries or yet to produce charges.</p><p>Trump named Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche as the acting attorney general, though three people familiar with the matter have said he has privately discussed Lee Zeldin, the head of the Environmental Protection Agency, as a permanent pick.</p><p>Bondi, a former Florida attorney general, <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pam-bondi-justice-department-trump-confirmation-7a37ef0b42964f9476776559379f48bd" target="_blank" >came into office</a> last year pledging that she would not play politics with the Justice Department, but she quickly started investigations of Trump foes, sparking an outcry that the law enforcement agency was being wielded as a tool of revenge to advance the president’s political and personal agenda.</p><p>She ushered in a period of intense turmoil at the department that included the firings of career prosecutors deemed insufficiently loyal to Trump and the resignations of hundreds of other employees. Her departure continues a trend of Justice Department upheaval that has defined Trump’s presidency as multiple attorneys general across his two terms have either been pushed out or resigned after proving unwilling or unable to meet his demands for the position.</p><p>Bondi rejected accusations that she politicized the Justice Department and said her mission was to restore the institution’s credibility after overreach by President Joe Biden’s Democratic administration with two federal criminal cases against Trump. Bondi’s defenders have said she worked to refocus the department to better tackle illegal immigration and violent crime and brought much-needed change to an agency they believe unfairly targeted conservatives.</p><h4>Embracing, supporting and protecting the president</h4><p>Bondi’s public embrace of the president, however, marked a sharp departure from her predecessors, who generally took pains to maintain an arm’s-length distance from the White House to protect the impartiality of investigations and prosecutions. Bondi postured herself as <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pam-bondi-house-judiciary-committee-justice-department-6d7502b80e42e9e9454264e242507bbd" target="_blank" >Trump’s chief supporter and protector</a>, praising and defending him in congressional hearings and placing a banner with his face on the exterior of Justice Department headquarters.</p><p>She called for an end to the “weaponization” of law enforcement she said occurred under the Biden administration, even though Biden’s attorney general, Merrick Garland, and Jack Smith, the special counsel who produced two cases against Trump, have said they followed the facts, the evidence and the law in their decision-making. Bondi’s critics, meanwhile, said she was the one who had politicized the agency to do the president’s bidding.</p><p>“You’ve turned the People’s Department of Justice into Trump’s instrument of revenge,” Rep. Jamie Raskin of Maryland, the top Democrat on the House Judiciary committee, said at a February hearing.</p><p>Bondi delivered a combative performance but few substantive answers at that hearing as she angrily insulted her Democratic questioners with name-calling, praised Trump over the performance of the stock market — “The Dow is up over 50,000 right now” —- and openly aligned herself as in sync with a president whom she painted as a victim of past impeachments and investigations.</p><p>Even Republicans began to challenge her, with the Republican-led House Oversight Committee last month <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/justice-department-epstein-bondi-subpoena-a3baffeaba386ee2e6e5041b067b83d3" target="_blank" >issuing a subpoena</a> to her to appear for a closed-door interview about the Epstein files.</p><p>Under Bondi’s leadership, the department opened investigations into a string of Trump foes, including Federal Reserve Chair <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-powell-federal-reserve-d87eedf1e35195957f903f9963aeaf99" target="_blank" >Jerome Powell</a>, New York Attorney General Letitia James, former FBI Director James Comey and former CIA Director <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/brennan-cia-trump-russia-justice-department-cannon-8272c2270987315fb39190a20d43dba0" target="_blank" >John Brennan</a>. The high-profile prosecutions of Comey and James were short-lived as they were quickly <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/comey-james-justice-department-5ec1a59d152bc1fd000ade15e20745b5" target="_blank" >thrown out</a> by a judge who ruled that the prosecutor who brought the cases was illegally appointed.</p><p>Trump repeatedly publicly praised and defended Bondi but also showed flashes of impatience with his attorney general’s efforts to meet his demands to prosecute his rivals. In one extraordinary social media post last year, Trump called on Bondi to move quickly to prosecute his foes, including James and Comey, telling her: “We can’t delay any longer, it’s killing our reputation and credibility.”</p><p>Bondi oversaw the exodus of thousands of career employees — both through firings and voluntary departures — including lawyers who prosecuted violent attacks on police at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021; environmental, civil rights and ethics enforcers; counterterrorism prosecutors; and others.</p><h4>Fumbling the Epstein files</h4><p>She struggled to overcome early stumbles over the Epstein files that angered conservatives eager for government bombshells about the case, which has long fascinated conspiracy theorists. She herself had fed the conspiracy theory machine with a suggestion in a 2025 Fox News Channel interview that Epstein’s “client list” was sitting on her desk for review. The department later acknowledged that no such document exists.</p><p>Bondi was ridiculed over a move to hand out <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/jeffrey-epstein-files-pam-bondi-trump-1a6af3e9fa1cfb6d267985a971a4929a" target="_blank" >binders of Epstein files</a> to conservative influencers at the White House only for it to be later revealed that the documents included no new revelations. And despite promises that more files were going to become public, the Justice Department in July said no more would be released, prompting Congress to pass a bill to force the agency to do so.</p><p>The Epstein files fumbles led to a stunning public criticism from White House chief of staff Susie Wiles, a close friend of Bondi’s, who told Vanity Fair that the attorney general <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-wiles-chief-staff-interview-cabinet-bondi-5c995a046318973b1a3dfe8ed89cf3cf" target="_blank" >“completely whiffed.”</a> The Justice Department’s release of millions of pages of Epstein files did little to tamp down criticism, prompting a House committee with the support of five Republicans to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/bondi-subpoena-epstein-files-house-committee-b16a5ab68c4a37a3a533e5f2412d7a57" target="_blank" >subpoena Bondi</a> to answer questions under oath.</p><p>Bondi, who defended Trump during his first impeachment trial, was his second choice to lead the Justice Department, picked for the role after former Rep. <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/matt-gaetz" target="_blank" >Matt Gaetz</a> of Florida withdrew his name from consideration amid scrutiny over sex trafficking allegations.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Habitantes de Chicago reaccionan a los argumentos sobre la ciudadanía por nacimiento ante la Corte Suprema
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON, D.C. — La Corte Suprema abordó uno de los casos más importantes del periodo, el decreto ejecutivo del presidente Donald Trump sobre la ciudadanía por nacimiento, que declara que los niños nacidos de padres que están en los Estados Unidos de manera ilegal o temporal no son ciudadanos estadounidenses.</p><p>En los argumentos del miércoles, los jueces escucharon la apelación de Trump contra un fallo de una corte menor en New Hampshire que anuló <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/protecting-the-meaning-and-value-of-american-citizenship/" target="_blank" >las restricciones de ciudadanía</a>, siendo una de varias cortes que las han bloqueado.</p><p>El procurador general D. John Sauer argumentó a favor del gobierno, enfrentándose a una pregunta escéptica tras otra. Los jueces indagaron sobre la base legal del decreto y expresaron preocupaciones más prácticas.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2b0000" name="module-2b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“¿Está pasando esto en la sala de partos?”, preguntó la jueza Ketanji Brown Jackson, profundizando en la logística de cómo el gobierno realmente determinaría quién tiene derecho a la ciudadanía y quién no.</p><p>El presidente de la Corte, John Roberts, sugirió que Sauer se apoyaba en excepciones peculiares a la ciudadanía para hacer un argumento amplio sobre las personas que están en el país de manera ilegal. “No estoy del todo seguro de cómo puedes llegar a ese gran grupo a partir de ejemplos tan pequeños y un tanto idiosincráticos”, dijo Roberts.</p><p>El juez Clarence Thomas parecía ser el más inclinado entre los nueve jueces a apoyar a Trump.</p><p>Se espera un fallo definitivo para principios del verano.</p><h3>El caso tiene nerviosa a la comunidad inmigrante</h3><p>Los líderes inmigrantes en Chicago dicen que están nerviosos por el caso y lo que el resultado podría significar para las familias de todo el país.</p><p>“Sentimos que hay este ataque constante [a los inmigrantes]”, dijo Grace Chan McKibben, directora ejecutiva de la Coalición por una Mejor Comunidad China Estadounidense.</p><p>Le preocupa que los hijos de inmigrantes puedan arriesgarse a perder beneficios como la atención médica o acceso a escuelas y otros recursos.</p><p>“Ciertamente, muchos de los programas que tenemos están abiertos tanto para ciudadanos como para titulares de tarjeta de residencia, residentes permanentes, pero no está claro si al rescindir la ciudadanía por nacimiento de los niños, ellos tendrían algún estatus”, dijo.</p><p>Trump fue el primer presidente en funciones en asistir a los argumentos orales en la corte más alta de la nación.</p><p>Ana Gil García, presidenta de la junta de la Alianza Venezolana de Illinois, dijo que la presencia de Trump en la sala de la corte estaba destinada a enviar un mensaje fuerte a los jueces que él nombró.</p><p>“La presencia del presidente con su esposa, que es inmigrante, en la corte hoy, es un elemento de intimidación”, dijo Garcia.</p><p>El caso enmarca otra prueba de las afirmaciones de Trump sobre el poder ejecutivo que desafían un precedente histórico en una corte que en gran medida ha fallado a favor del presidente, pero con algunas excepciones notables a las que Trump ha respondido con críticas profundamente personales hacia los jueces.</p><p>El decreto de Trump alteraría la visión de muchos años que la 14 Enmienda de la Constitución, ratificada en 1868, y la ley federal desde 1940, confiere ciudadanía a todos los nacidos en suelo estadounidense, con excepciones limitadas para los hijos de diplomáticos extranjeros y aquellos nacidos bajo una fuerza ocupante extranjera.</p><p>La 14 Enmienda fue diseñada para asegurar que las personas negras, incluidas las que fueron esclavas, tuvieran ciudadanía, aunque la Cláusula de Ciudadanía está redactada de manera más amplia. “Todas las personas nacidas o naturalizadas en los Estados Unidos, y sujetas a la jurisdicción de los mismos, son ciudadanos de los Estados Unidos y del Estado en el que residen”, dice.</p><p>En una serie de decisiones, las cortes de menor instancia han anulado el decreto ejecutivo como ilegal, o probablemente ilegal, bajo la Constitución y la ley federal. Las decisiones han invocado el fallo de la Corte Suprema de 1898 en Wong Kim Ark, que sostuvo que el niño nacido en Estados Unidos de ciudadanos chinos era un ciudadano.</p><p>La administración de Trump argumenta que la visión común de la ciudadanía es incorrecta, afirmando que los hijos de no ciudadanos no están “sujetos a la jurisdicción” de los Estados Unidos y, por lo tanto, no tienen derecho a la ciudadanía. La corte debería utilizar el caso para corregir “malentendidos de mucho tiempo sobre el significado de la Constitución”, escribió el procurador general D. John Sauer.</p><p>Ninguna corte ha aceptado ese argumento, y los abogados de mujeres embarazadas cuyos hijos se verían afectados por el decreto dijeron que la Corte Suprema no debería ser la primera en hacerlo. “Tenemos al presidente de los Estados Unidos tratando de reinterpretar radicalmente la definición de ciudadanía estadounidense”, dijo Cecillia Wang, la directora legal de la Unión Estadounidense por las Libertades Civiles (ACLU), quien se enfrentó a Sauer en la Corte Suprema.</p><p>Más de 250,000 bebés nacidos en Estados Unidos cada año se verían afectados por el decreto ejecutivo, según una investigación del Instituto de Política Migratoria y el Instituto de Investigación de Población de la Universidad Estatal de Pensilvania.</p><p>García dice que el argumento de la administración de Trump de que las mujeres embarazadas vienen a Estados Unidos para aprovecharse de la ciudadanía por nacimiento es injusto y no refleja las verdaderas razones que llevan a los inmigrantes venezolanos a emigrar a los Estados Unidos.</p><p>“Las familias inmigrantes vinieron escapando de la pobreza, de la violencia, de condiciones de vida inhumanas, del terrorismo”, dijo García.</p><p>Negar la ciudadanía a los hijos nacidos de venezolanos que carecen de estatus legal creará una segunda clase de niños apátridas, agregó.</p><p>“No serán de aquí, no serán de allá”, dijo García. “No serán de ninguna parte”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" >La Voz Chicago</a></p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Man accused of making bomb threat that forced Chicago-bound flight to land in Michigan, feds say
entries.summary: <p>An Illinois man was charged with making a fake bomb threat that diverted a Chicago-bound American Airlines flight to Detroit, where it made an emergency landing over the weekend. </p><p>A federal criminal complaint filed in the Eastern District of Michigan claims Jason Douglas Pazol "engaged in conduct with the intent to convey false or misleading information."</p><p>Pazol, charged with making a false bomb threat, was on board flight AA2819 from New York with over 150 other passengers and crew Sunday when <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/30/unruly-passenger-apparently-caused-chicago-bound-american-airlines-flight-to-divert-to-detroit" target="_blank" >he “loudly threatened” to blow the flight up, according to the complaint. </a></p><p>Pazol is listed as a teacher at Kildeer Countryside Elementary in suburban Long Grove, according to their website. The school didn’t immediately verify whether Pazol was still employed there when reached by the Sun-Times.</p><p>Reviewed video by an FBI agent shows Pazol with his head in his hands yelling, "If someone even near me, I swear to God, I am going to do something terrible. Land this f - - - - - - plane, right now.”</p><p>A woman who possibly was Pazol's wife was talking to aircraft personnel about his medication when the yelling started. </p><p>The complaint states Pazol continued yelling things like "I know I’m paranoid. So you better not be near me" and "I will blow this f - - - - - - plane up. And you heard it, you heard it everybody. You heard me say that, I want this plane on the ground.”</p><p>Flight personnel then went into "bomb threat protocol” and made an emergency landing at Detroit Metropolitan Airport in Romulus, Michigan, authorities said. Pazol was then detained and taken to a hospital for evaluation.</p><p>After Pazol was detained, he admitted to threatening to blow up the plane and told FBI agents he had “racing thoughts” and had a breakdown on the plane after having problems with his medication, officials said. He added that he made the threat "to force the aircraft to land" because "he felt passengers weren't safe around him." </p><p>A search confirmed that no explosives were found on the plane, any luggage or any passengers on the plane, the complaint states.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Connor Murphy, Jason Dickinson experiencing stark contrast between Blackhawks' rebuild, Oilers' win-now focus
entries.summary: <p>EDMONTON, Alberta — Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> stalwarts Connor Murphy and Jason Dickinson are currently enjoying something they haven't experienced in a long time: Winning.</p><p>The Oilers went 9-4-1 in March, including 8-4-1 once Murphy and Dickinson joined their lineup. Remarkably, that was Murphy's winningest month since December 2019.</p><p>That's right: the Hawks haven't won eight games in a single calendar month in over six years.</p><p>So neither Murphy nor Dickinson can deny that it's fun to be on the top side of the often frustratingly stagnant NHL hierarchy after spending so long on the bottom. There's something to be said for instant gratification.</p><p>"Winning feels so good," Murphy said Thursday. "Losing is very hard. Over the years in Chicago, it eats at you, having a lot of losses. After a while, you're quick to question yourself and your team and your game and what needs to change. </p><p>"[When you] put together an effort that you think is worthy of a win and then you’re able to <i>get</i> that result, it definitely feeds confidence."</p><p>Both veterans were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/28/blackhawks-connor-murphy-reflects-on-tenure-chicago-home-patience" target="_blank" >exemplary teammates</a> during their lengthy Chicago tenures, never letting their frustrations affect themselves or others. Murphy explained Thursday he was always focused on making the most out of whatever they had.</p><p>And they understood why the Hawks' situation was the way it was. They weren't ignorant to the empty cupboard that general manager Kyle Davidson inherited in 2022 from Stan Bowman, now their GM in Edmonton. Davidson also kept them, as alternate captains, loosely in the loop about his rebuilding strategy and plans.</p><p>"You've got to accept that sometimes [when] you invest heavily into a generation, the next generation is going to suffer for it," Dickinson said. "I don't know if there's a way to work around it. Some teams have tried to go with retool after retool and find no success. It [becomes] a constant cycle of being a middling team that doesn't end up doing anything important. </p><p>"Is that worth it for the fan base to have a group that just goes out there and plays good hockey forever? Or do you want to see some years of exceptional games and fantastic runs that really create the memories and legacy of an organization?"</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6d0000" name="image-6d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fd8a15f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5004x2808+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Ffc%2F33d165c9440ca5d32e916b6ad073%2Fkraken-oilers-hockey.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de0931c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5004x2808+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Ffc%2F33d165c9440ca5d32e916b6ad073%2Fkraken-oilers-hockey.jpg"
