alt="Kraken Oilers Hockey" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de0931c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5004x2808+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Ffc%2F33d165c9440ca5d32e916b6ad073%2Fkraken-oilers-hockey.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c6b8f8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5004x2808+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Ffc%2F33d165c9440ca5d32e916b6ad073%2Fkraken-oilers-hockey.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de0931c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5004x2808+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Ffc%2F33d165c9440ca5d32e916b6ad073%2Fkraken-oilers-hockey.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Murphy and Dickinson have already enjoyed success with the Oilers.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jason Franson/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a few years, too, the Oilers' outlook may look as bleak as the Hawks' outlook used to be. Bowman is doing what he does, consistently trading picks for prime-aged players, and that approach will eventually exhaust the basin. </p><p>Even acquiring Murphy and Dickinson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/04/blackhawks-oilers-trade-jason-dickinson-colton-dach-draft-pick" target="_blank" >cost a pretty penny</a>, with the Hawks receiving first- and second-round picks in exchange. The Oilers have of the league's worst prospect pools and now own only three picks in the first four rounds of the 2026 and 2027 drafts combined.</p><p>For a franchise with just two guaranteed years of Connor McDavid left, however, it makes sense to be going all-in every year. </p><p>The Oilers haven't been as dominant overall this season as in the last four, but the Pacific Division's ineptitude offers them a relatively straightforward path back to the Western Conference Final, at the least.</p><p>Besides, Murphy and Dickinson aren't thinking much about the future (especially as pending free agents). They've waited years for a bite at the playoff apple, and they plan to make it count.</p><p>"Every day, I’ve just tried to take [things] as serious and focused as I can, knowing the end goal — to get to the playoffs and push for a run — will be a dream come true," Murphy said.</p><p>Said Dickinson: "This is the best time of the year, and for it to be meaningful and matter is huge. The last few years, I've spent it just counting down the days, waiting for summer to start, knowing that it was back to the gym...to prepare for the next season. Now it's [time to] start preparing for the next chapter of the season."</p><h3><b>Perfect fits</b></h3><p>People in and around the Oilers organization raved Thursday about how effectively Murphy and Dickinson have rounded out the roster and improved the team's defense.</p><p>"We wanted to get better defensively, and we have gotten better defensively," Oilers coach Kris Knoblauch said. "We added two very detailed defensive players, Dickinson and Murphy, who are playing a lot of minutes for us against key matchups. Part of it is player personnel. Those two have really solidified our identity of how we want to play."</p><p>Both Murphy and Dickinson knew trades were coming, but the fact they had never been traded mid-season before nonetheless led to a jarring transition.</p><p>"You just jump into a group that's already playing and they've already got all their systems in place," Dickinson said. "You're not trying to figure it out together. I'm jumping right into the fire."</p><p>They've since settled in, though. Murphy has formed a shutdown pairing with Darnell Nurse, helping Nurse stabilize his game, while Dickinson has assumed a familiar role as a checking center alongside Adam Henrique.</p><p>Before the trades, the Oilers ranked 23rd in the NHL in scoring chances allowed per minute of five-on-five play. Since the trades (entering Thursday), they rank eighth.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Outgoing Chicago inspector general’s parting shot to Mayor Johnson – he’s 'reflexively hostile to oversight'
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson has shown himself to be “reflexively hostile to oversight” and has “not lived up to” his campaign promise to govern as a reformer, outgoing Inspector General Deborah Witzburg said Thursday.</p><p>Witzburg has clashed with the Johnson administration on a host of issues — from the mayor’s decision to deny her investigators access to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/11/04/city-inspector-general-deborah-witzburg-mayor-brandon-johnson-gift-room" >City Hall gift room</a> to the Law Department’s demand to water down ethics reforms and sit in on IG interviews with city employees.</p><p>Johnson also rejected Witzburg’s demand that he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/10/15/city-inspector-general-deborah-witzburg-brandon-johnson-top-adviser-jason-lee-investigation" >fire senior mayoral adviser Jason Lee</a> for refusing to cooperate with her investigation of an alleged quid pro quo threat that Lee made to Ald. Bill Conway (34th) in 2023.</p><p>The relationship between Johnson and Witzburg was fraught, minimizing any chance that the mayor would reappoint her. </p><p>On Thursday, Witzburg pulled no punches as she evaluated the most progressive mayor in Chicago history on his track record for embracing ethics and good government.</p><p>“This administration has shown itself to be reflexively hostile to oversight. This has come largely through the Law Department… interfering with OIG’s access to city premises, withholding records from OIG, declining to provide records even though they were, in fact, publicly available, declining to implement recommendations from OIG,” Witzburg told the Sun-Times.</p><p>“Reasonable minds can differ on facts, and even on the law. But we are entitled to, and we ought to see, this course of conduct out of City Hall for what it is — a pattern of things."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/19/mayor-johnson-deputy-mayor-of-public-safety-garien-gatewood-firing-toxic-work-environment" >Garien Gatewood</a> claims he was fired as deputy mayor for community safety by Lee and chief of staff Cristina Pacione-Zayas in apparent retaliation for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/24/brandon-johnson-garien-gatewood-firing-inspector-general-deborah-witzburg-complaint" >complaint he filed</a> with the inspector general’s office in October accusing them of improper interference in City Hall’s contracting process. The same sources said Johnson was made aware of those contract interference concerns.</p><p>Witzburg refused to comment on that investigation, nor would she say whether it involves the long-awaited search for a replacement to ShotSpotter, the gunshot detection technology contract that Johnson terminated shortly after taking office.</p><p>But Witzburg appeared to echo Gatewood's concern about being "targeted" when she said, "The thing we ought to be cautious about is whether city personnel processes and city legal processes are being weaponized to protect political allies and go after opponents."</p><p>The mayor's office pushed back, saying the inspector general's office may be "using its platform to air politicized grievances in a manner that risks compromising the credibility and independence" of an office that "must remain grounded in facts, fairness and the public trust."</p><p>Johnson "remains deeply committed to good governance and public transparency" and has "worked in good faith to implement and abide by" the watered-down ethics reforms that Witzburg negotiated with the Law Department last summer, the mayor's office statement said.</p><p>Two years ago, Johnson was forced to restore 162 police jobs tied to implementing the consent decree after Illinois Attorney General Kwame Raoul threatened to ask a federal judge to hold the city in contempt.</p><p>On Thursday, Witzburg said it’s time for the city to “fish or cut bait” on a consent decree that, seven years in, is “somewhere between a fifth and a quarter” fully implemented.</p><p>“That bodes for a very long and very expensive road... if we continue to move this slowly,” Witzburg said.</p><p>“If you’re pedaling your bicycle too slowly, the bicycle tips over… That is sort of where we are… If we are going to be serious about getting out from under the consent decree, then the department needs adequate resources to make the appropriate changes."</p><p>As chief watchdog, Witzburg is also tasked with investigating waste and fraud in city government, and recommending ways the city could operate more efficiently.</p><p>She argued that Johnson has come up short on that front. Although he "inherited a deeply compromised fiscal landscape," the mayor "owns everything that has happened for the last three years," Witzburg said.</p><p>"When we look at the rapidly growing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/25/debts-unpaid-fines-chicago-city-hall-mayor-brandon-johnson" >uncollected debt</a> [and] at the fractious and damaging process that went on with the City Council during the<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/12/23/mayor-brandon-johnson-city-council-alternate-budget" > budget season,</a> the historical legacy is what it is," she said.</p><p>Witzburg will conclude her one-and-done, four-year term on April 24. A nationwide search for her replacement has been under way for months.</p><p>She refused to disclose her next move, but said she is "not going far."</p><p>"I will steal a phrase from Rahm Emanuel. We heard him say recently he is not done with public service and he hopes public service isn't done with him," Witzburg said. "I have spent my whole career as a government lawyer. I have only ever intended to work in public service. That continues to be my plan."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-360000" name="html-embed-module-360000"></a><iframe allow="monetization" frameborder="0" height="200" scrolling="no" src="https://play.prx.org/e?ge=prx_12963_931b27d7-eaac-4b65-a3e1-be08ae794d7a&uf=https%3A%2F%2Fpublicfeeds.net%2Ff%2F12963%2Ffeed-rss.xml" style="min-width: 300px;" width="100%"> </iframe> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Former Savannah Banana Tristan Peters lining up his shot in White Sox outfield
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — There’s nothing smooth about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> outfielder Tristan Peters’ game.</p><p>“Gritty,” he calls it. “I’m going to run hard every time, try to beat out infield singles if I can and put my body on the line if I need to, just to make those catches.”</p><p>But it turns out Peters is pretty smooth on the line, when he wants to be. Line dancing, country and swing, that is.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-right> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" height="500" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/66b19fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1062x1416+79+0/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F95%2F43%2F85c48d5348fb903602b0b3b5d33f%2Fsoxmain43.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tristan Peters" width="375" height="500" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/66b19fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1062x1416+79+0/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F95%2F43%2F85c48d5348fb903602b0b3b5d33f%2Fsoxmain43.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tristan Peters</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Manitoba native found his groove when he wasn’t turning heads in center field at Chandler-Gilbert Community College in Arizona in 2019. His skillset both on and off the field stuck with an opposing junior-college manager, who went on to coach the Savannah Bananas and recruit Peters to the famed exhibition team.</p><p>As the 26-year-old Peters now tries to stake his big-league claim in the Sox’ outfield, he credits his brief foray into Banana Ball with helping to develop the high-octane, free-wheeling attitude he brings to the field.</p><p>“It's not really my type of thing, but it helped me a lot just to get out of my shell,” said Peters, who also met his wife, Erin, on the Arizona line-dancing circuit. “And I honestly had a blast playing for them.”</p><p>The Savannah squad that Peters played for in 2021 wasn’t quite the choreographed touring spectacle that packed Rate Field <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-culture/2025/08/14/savannah-bananas-guaranteed-rate-field" target="_blank" ><u>last year</u></a> and comes to Wrigley Field in July. He went 16-for-50 with four doubles across 17 games that could be considered collegiate amateur contests with regular dance interludes.</p><p>“It was still like regular baseball, but I enjoyed it very much. Made some good friends, too. They kind of pulled whatever they could out of you. I did do two dances, which I never would have seen myself doing before that,” Peters said.</p><p>He politely declined a demonstration during his first road trip with the Sox, but videos of his kilted ensemble performances — including a final one to the tune of Britney Spears’ “Baby One More Time” — aren’t difficult to track down online.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">The Final Dance in BananaLand for Tristan Peters 🥲 <br><br>The Brewers got themselves a good one. <a href="https://t.co/KLP5IGZIo1">pic.twitter.com/KLP5IGZIo1</a></p>— Savannah Bananas (@TheSavBananas) <a href="https://twitter.com/TheSavBananas/status/1415452514133098496?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">July 14, 2021</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>“It just brings up some of the more fun aspects of the game, especially for kids. They love that showy kind of stuff,” said Peters, who also played at Southern Illinois University and got drafted by the Brewers while wearing Banana yellow.</p><p>Acquired by the Sox from Tampa Bay <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/12/18/white-sox-acquire-outfielder-tristan-peters-from-rays" target="_blank" ><u>in December</u></a> for cash considerations, Peters showed out with a .270/.333/.514 spring training, including two doubles, two triples and a homer to grab a roster spot.</p><p>“It was good at-bats, grindy-type at bats, which we love to see,” assistant general manager Josh Barfield said. “He's got some juice, but he can put down a bunt. It's just a fun style of baseball to watch.”</p><p>After appearing in four games last season with the Rays, Peters knocked the first hit of his career last weekend in Milwaukee, the start of a five-game on-base streak. He also swiped his first career stolen base Tuesday in Miami and headed to Chicago hitting 5-for-15 with a double after the Sox’ <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/munetaka-murakami-chicago-white-sox-debut" target="_blank" ><u>god-awful first two series</u></a> of the season.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/21/chicago-snowballs-savannah-bananas-rocket-squirrels-cherie-travis" >Chicago’s own Savannah Banana-style exhibition baseball teams are rolling out</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“Tristan can get you in a lot of different ways,” manager Will Venable said. “Gives our offense a little bit of a different look and a different weapon in there. … Really quality at-bats, really competitive and the defense is really good, too.”</p><p>Peters knows as well as anyone that nothing’s guaranteed for him with regular turnover this year between the Sox and Triple-A Charlotte, especially with injured utility player Brooks Baldwin <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/13/white-sox-outfielder-brooks-baldwin-sidelined-by-right-elbow-inflammation" target="_blank" ><u>on the mend</u></a> and infield prospect Sam Antonacci <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/28/white-sox-prospects-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci-triple-a-opening-day?666" target="_blank" ><u>taking regular reps in left field</u></a> to hasten his path to The Show.</p><p>Peters will just keep lining it up as long as they let him.</p><p>“It's always a blessing to be in big leagues,” Peters said. “Just trying to make the most of it. Having been with so many teams, it is kind of cool.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, items=[{type=Image, category=[{items=[Sports Media]}], _styledTemplate=null, imageAlignment=null, isFeatured=false, publishDate=April 01, 2026 04:00 PM, url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/04/01/mlb-revives-this-week-in-baseball, authors=[{authorPageUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/associated-press, media=[{narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/d1/88/7112e7cfa94944d87224e36ff8ff/ap-icon-512x512-0.png, width=512, height=512}, image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/d1/88/7112e7cfa94944d87224e36ff8ff/ap-icon-512x512-0.png, width=512, height=512}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=ap-icon-512x512-0.png}], ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, bio=[{items=[The Associated Press is an independent, not-for-profit news cooperative headquartered in New York City. entries.title: Flooding near O'Hare reduces inbound I-90 to single lane
entries.summary: <p>Storms passing through the Chicago area on Thursday afternoon caused flooding on the interstate near O'Hare Airport.</p><p>Inbound I-190 was reduced to one lane near the Mannheim exit, according to a social media <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/fly2ohare/status/2039790903649272260" target="_blank" >post</a> from O'Hare's X account.</p><p>Officials advised travelers to allow extra time to commute or consider public transit. The Illinois Department of Transportation was on scene to address the issue.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Three arrested after ATF agent opens fire at robbery suspect in Pullman; no one hurt
entries.summary: <p>An agent from the the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms opened fire at a robbery suspect Thursday morning on the Far South Side. </p><p>It happened during an ATF operation in the CPD 5th district near the 109000 block of South Doty Avenue, Mark Kavanagh, an ATF spokesperson, said in an email. </p><p>The agent discharged their gun at a suspect during an attempted robbery, he said, citing preliminary information. </p><p>No law enforcement personnel or suspects were injured but three suspects are in custody, and charges are pending, according to Kavanagh. </p><p>Chicago police referred inquiries about the shooting to ATF.</p><p><i>Check back for details on this developing story. </i><br></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Illini great Deon Thomas says former coach Bruce Pearl doesn't belong on TV — and he's right
entries.summary: <p>For Illini fans of a certain age, the men’s basketball team’s delightful run to the Final Four has had a tinge of irritation.</p><p>During the TBS broadcasts of Illinois’ Sweet 16 and Elite Eight games last week, the studio panel included a villainous figure in Illini history. Some would say he was more reviled than the greatest villain of them all, “The General,” former Indiana coach Bobby Knight.</p><p>His last name rhymes with “hurl”: former Iowa assistant Bruce Pearl.</p><p>His mere presence on our screens — yes, I’m an Illinois grad, Class of ’96 — has drawn our ire. Seeing Pearl on the set with Illinois and Iowa logos pictured behind him was a jarring sight. But none of us was even the victim of Pearl’s deceitful, unconscionable behavior during a recruiting competition in 1989. Imagine being that person and seeing Pearl on the screen.</p><p>Imagine being Deon Thomas.</p><p>“You get a little triggered,” Thomas said.</p><p>Thomas, in his 10th year as Illinois’ lead radio analyst, was Pearl’s target. Pearl secretly recorded a conversation in which he asked Thomas if he had been offered $80,000 and a Chevy Blazer by Illinois assistant Jimmy Collins to sign with the school. Though he had received nothing, the Simeon star didn’t offer a denial, and Pearl sent the tape to the NCAA.</p><p>Though nothing came of the allegations, the NCAA found other infractions and banned Illinois from the 1991 postseason. Thomas couldn’t play as a freshman — though he went on to become the school’s all-time scoring leader — Collins’ reputation suffered and coaching great Lou Henson never duplicated the success of his ’89 Flyin’ Illini team, which made the Final Four.</p><p>So, yeah, “triggered.”</p><p>“But I really do my best not to think of that person,” Thomas said, “because if I allow him to occupy any space in my brain, it’s not gonna be positive for me.”</p><p>Pearl, who apologized to Thomas in 2011 — “As I told him, it’s about 25 years too late, but OK,” Thomas said — joined CBS Sports in October, shortly after stepping down as Auburn’s coach. He cited his desire to spend more time with family and to hand the reins to his associate head coach and son Steven. More on that later.</p><p>Despite the apology, Pearl never dropped his devious ways. In 2010, the SEC suspended Pearl, then Tennessee’s coach, for eight games for recruiting violations. The school fired him in 2011, but only because it couldn’t overlook his offenses anymore. In 2021, the NCAA put his Auburn program on four years’ probation and suspended him for two games for compliance failures.</p><p>“I am a person of second chances,” Thomas said. “But I don’t understand why you should get a third, a fourth, a fifth.</p><p>“I think character matters. So you ask if I think he should be on television? No.”</p><p>Pearl has won everywhere he has been: two NCAA Tournament appearances with Milwaukee, six with Tennessee and six with Auburn. He clearly can coach, and he has charisma. But that checkered past hovers over him, and his own words have caused problems for him.</p><p>After saying in February that a then-undefeated Miami (Ohio) team didn’t deserve a tournament bid, he changed his tune in March and predicted the RedHawks would get in after hearing blowback. He also stumped for Auburn, his son’s team, prompting Miami athletics director David Sayler to suggest that CBS should’ve put a disclaimer on the screen.</p><p>“You can’t be on television advocating for your son against other programs that have every right to have an opportunity to get into the NCAA Tournament,” Thomas said.</p><p>Thomas is right on all counts, but it won’t stop Pearl from appearing on Final Four programming Saturday around the Illinois-UConn game. Fortunately for Thomas, he won’t have to watch. He’ll be calling the game alongside play-by-play voice Brian Barnhart on the Illini Sports Network, which includes WLS-AM (890).</p><p>Thomas sees a much different game unfolding between the teams than the one they played in November at Madison Square Garden, a 74-61 Huskies victory.</p><p>“This is a different Illinois team,” Thomas said. “[Tomislav Ivisic] was just coming off of having his tonsils removed [and a knee injury]. Keaton Wagler was not the same Keaton Wagler that he is currently. That team was nowhere near the team that it is now defensively.</p><p>“They had so many moving parts that were new. That early in the season, you’re really trying to learn and figure out who you are. Now they know.”</p><p>Thomas isn’t surprised that the episode with Pearl keeps coming up — “People like drama,” he said — but he won’t let it stop him from enjoying his first Final Four experience. He was too young for the 1989 team, and he was still playing professionally for Maccabi Tel Aviv in Israel when the 2005 team reached the championship game.</p><p>“Life is good,” said Thomas, who doubles as the athletic department’s director of major gifts out of the school’s Chicago office. “I tell people on daily basis, I may have a job, but I don’t work.”</p><h3>Remote patrol</h3><p>CBS 2 will air a 30-minute Illini special at 6:30 p.m. Friday, “CBS Center Court: Road to the Championship.” <b>Ryan Baker</b> and <b>Jermont Terry</b>, both Illinois alums, will report live from Indianapolis, and <b>Matt Zahn</b> will anchor from the studio.</p><p>• Big Ten Network will air three live, on-site shows Saturday around the Final Four: “B1G Live: Final Four Preview” at 11 a.m., “B1G Live: Final Four Pregame” at 4 p.m. and “The B1G Show” at about 10:30 p.m., after the Michigan-Arizona game.</p><p>• Chicago Sports Network will air an hourlong pregame show for the White Sox’ home opener Friday starting at noon.</p><p>• The Cubs-Guardians game Saturday will air at 6:15 p.m. on Fox 32. <b>Adam Amin</b>, <b>John Smoltz</b> and <b>Ken Rosenthal</b> will call it.</p><h3>FINAL FOUR</h3><p><b>TV:</b> TBS, TNT, truTV.</p><p><b>Streaming:</b> NCAA March Madness Live, HBO Max.</p><p><b>Lineup</b></p><p><b>2 p.m. —</b> “The Final Four Show” with host Adam Lefkoe and analysts Jamal Mashburn, Candace Parker, Jalen Rose and Chris Webber.</p><p><b>3 p.m. —</b> “At the Final Four” with host Ernie Johnson and analysts Charles Barkley, Clark Kellogg, Kenny Smith and Bruce Pearl.</p><p><b>5:09 p.m. —</b> Illinois vs. UConn with Ian Eagle, analysts Grant Hill and Bill Raftery and reporter Tracy Wolfson. Michigan vs. Arizona follows.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Acusan a estudiante de DePaul de escupir a agente de ICE en O'Hare, pero abogado dice que el caso es exagerado
entries.summary: <p>Un estudiante de la Universidad DePaul fue acusado de escupir a un agente del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) que trabajaba en el Aeropuerto O’Hare, en medio de un cierre parcial del gobierno provocado por llamados a reformar ICE.</p><p>Brett Heier, de 20 años, "intencional y forzosamente" escupió al agente el domingo por la tarde al salir de la Terminal 2 en O’Hare, según un informe de arresto de la Policía de Chicago. Heier enfrenta un cargo de delito grave de agresión agravada a un oficial de la paz.</p><p>Pero su abogado, Barry Sheppard, argumentó que su cliente sólo es acusado de escupir en el zapato del agente y que podría haber sido involuntario. El informe de arresto señala que Heier estaba tomando medicamentos para un absceso dental.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-af0000" name="module-af0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"En el pasado, [ICE] parece haber hecho de incidentes menores algo más grande de lo que es", dijo Sheppard en una entrevista, "y siento que este también fue exagerado".</p><p>Un portavoz de ICE dijo que sus empleados "están lidiando con un aumento del 1,300% en ataques, un aumento del 3,200% en ataques con vehículos y un sorprendente aumento del 8,000% en amenazas de muerte mientras arriesgan sus vidas para arrestar a asesinos, violadores, pedófilos y miembros de pandillas".</p><p>El agente de ICE que supuestamente fue golpeado por el escupitajo identificó la foto de Heier en una rueda de reconocimiento, al igual que un segundo funcionario de la agencia. La Policía de Chicago arrestó a Heier el martes al salir de su apartamento en Lincoln Park, y se ordenó que fuera liberado de la custodia durante su primera audiencia judicial el miércoles.</p><p>El supuesto incidente de escupitajo en O’Hare ocurrió una semana después de que el presidente Donald Trump anunciara que estaba <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/despliegan-a-agentes-de-ice-en-ohare-mientras-continua-el-cierre-parcial-del-gobierno-que-afecta-a-viajeros" target="_blank" >enviando agentes de ICE a aeropuertos</a> en todo el país para ayudar con la seguridad en medio de la lucha por la financiación del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS).</p><p>El cierre resultante del gobierno ha llevado a largas filas en los aeropuertos y problemas de personal entre los agentes de la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA), quienes no recibieron pago desde mediados de febrero hasta que Trump firmó una orden ejecutiva que les otorgó pago retroactivo la semana pasada.</p><p>Los empleados de otras agencias de DHS, incluidos ICE y la Aduana y Protección Fronteriza (CBP), han continuado recibiendo pago porque están siendo financiados por el proyecto de ley de política nacional de Trump aprobado durante el verano.</p><p>El cierre fue provocado por los llamados no respondidos de los demócratas para reformas después de que dos ciudadanos estadounidenses fueron asesinados durante una agresiva campaña de deportación en Minnesota a principios de este año.</p><p>Mientras tanto, la Fiscal del Estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neill Burke, ha enfrentado llamados para investigar y procesar a los agentes de inmigración que llevaron a cabo un operativo similar en el área de Chicago. Ha dicho que está legalmente limitada cuando se trata de investigar a las autoridades federales, incluso mientras se han lanzado esfuerzos legales para instalar un fiscal especial en su oficina para hacerlo.</p><p>Un portavoz de la fiscal del estado se negó a comentar sobre el caso de Heier y la decisión de acusarlo de un delito grave.</p><p>El abogado de Heier dijo que las acciones que se alegan de su cliente caen bajo el "amplio espectro de la libertad de expresión", y agregó, "es un joven muy bueno que le va bien en la universidad, nunca ha tenido ningún tipo de arresto antes".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Frank Main

entries.title: Carla Berube brings March expertise to Northwestern women's basketball. Will the university meet her there?
entries.summary: <p>New Northwestern women's basketball coach Carla Berube is used to winning. And not just the regular season-grind kind of winning, but the kind that keeps teams dancing through March.</p><p>At Princeton, where she coached the last six seasons, she led the Tigers to five straight NCAA Tournament appearances, advancing to the second round in 2022 and 2023. Her record in March — spanning conference tournaments and the Big Dance — was a staggering 21-6.</p><p>Before that, she helped transform Tufts, a Division III school, into a national powerhouse. In her 17 seasons, Tufts made the Sweet 16 nine times and played in the national championship game twice.</p><p>“I love to win,” Berube said at her introductory news conference in Evanston on Tuesday. “I’m lucky enough to be doing that a lot. I think we can do it here.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/25/northwestern-carla-berube-womens-basketball-coach-uconn-princeton" >Northwestern hires Princeton’s Carla Berube as women’s basketball coach</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Berube’s history gives her natural confidence. But it’s fair to wonder if it will translate at Northwestern.</p><p>Since 2000, the Wildcats have made only two NCAA Tournament appearances. They claimed a Big Ten regular-season title in 2020, then dropped to the bottom of the conference standings in the four seasons since.</p><p>The decline reflects a sluggishness to adapt to a new, more professionalized college basketball environment. Players can now profit off their name, image, and likeness (NIL), luring stars to schools that can offer lucrative deals. They can also transfer without penalty, meaning player movement is more common than ever.</p><p>These dynamics will be new to Berube. The Ivy League, where she coached at Princeton, operates differently. There are no athletic scholarships, and incoming transfers to the conference are rare. </p><p>The good news is Berube recognizes she’s not in Kansas anymore. She's in the mighty Big Ten, and is already adjusting her game plan.</p><p>“We’ll definitely be using the [transfer] portal,” Berube said. “We’ve got to bring in some talented student-athletes from the portal to supplement the great student-athletes that we have here.”</p><p>But Berube’s success won't just be about her own adaptability. It depends on whether the university invests behind her. Former coach Joe McKeown, who just retired after 18 seasons, was candid about Northwestern’s hesitancy to participate in the NIL era.</p><p>“We really haven’t been that involved in putting money out there for players,” he told the Sun-Times in January 2025. “Other people have.”</p><p>He thought this was rooted in institutional pride: wanting players to come to Northwestern for the degree and the experience, not for the money. He also acknowledged they needed to be more active with NIL to survive in the Big Ten.</p><p>So, is Northwestern actually ready to pay up?</p><p>Publicly, the signals are there. New athletic director Mark Jackson has established positions like a chief revenue officer and a chief communications and branding officer within the athletic department. And Berube insists she wouldn’t have left a winning situation if she didn’t believe that Northwestern was stepping up.</p><p>Yet, the skepticism among those already in the building is palpable. Less than a month ago, following a season-ending loss to Purdue, men’s basketball coach Chris Collins called for more investment.</p><p>“It’s a big business now,” Collins said. “You’ve got to invest … it’s got to trickle down from the administration to the coaching staff. Everybody’s got to be in alignment in order to win because the programs that are winning, that’s what they’re doing. They’re aligned. There’s a commitment to winning with resources, with investment.”</p><p>Those comments suggest that Northwestern has yet to fully put its money where its mouth is.</p><p>If the administration follows through, though, Berube has good reason to be hopeful. Especially because there’s a Big Ten turnaround story playing out just 150 miles south in Champaign.</p><p>In coach Shauna Green’s first season, Illinois leaped from the conference cellar to a 22-10 record and their first tournament appearance in two decades. Green immediately landed high-impact transfers, then brought in stellar freshmen classes to elevate them further.</p><p>Berube is shooting for something similar: an NCAA Tournament appearance in her first season.</p><p>“I want to be playing in March,” she said. “And in mid-March.”</p><p>Berube has proven she knows the way to the tournament. Now Northwestern has to decide if it's willing to pay for the trip.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Brasileño que ha corrido por las calles de Chicago enfrenta la expiración de su visa de estudiante
entries.summary: <p>Después de mudarse a Chicago, Joabe Barbosa se propuso sumergirse en su nuevo hogar.</p><p>Pero ahora Barbosa, quien ha llamado la atención por <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/13/chicago-student-running-chicago" target="_blank" >correr por casi todas las calles de Chicago</a> vistiendo la bandera de la ciudad como una capa, puede no poder quedarse.</p><p>Barbosa se mudó a Chicago en 2022, inscribiéndose en la Universidad Roosevelt para obtener un doctorado en psicología clínica. Está en camino de terminar su carrera en 2027, pero después de no conseguir una pasantía en su último año, este joven de 25 años, que está en Estados Unidos con una visa F-1, corre el riesgo de ser forzado a abandonar el país y regresar a su Brasil natal.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c40000" name="module-c40000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Ha sido frustrante”, dijo Barbosa. “Gastamos tanto dinero para estudiar y es frustrante saber que no hay muchas opciones para que los estudiantes internacionales se queden y terminen sus títulos”.</p><p>Barbosa, que creció en Londres, dijo que su familia ha adquirido deudas para ayudar a cubrir el costo de su educación, y volver a Brasil solo aumentaría su carga financiera. Su padre ha iniciado un <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/help-my-son-continue-his-journey-to-becoming-a-psychologist" target="_blank" >GoFundMe</a> para ayudar a Barbosa a cubrir el costo del viaje. La página ha recaudado más de $5,300.</p><p>Dijo que tendrá 60 días después de que termine el año escolar para abandonar el país, lo que requerirá que regrese a Brasil y espere hasta el próximo año para volver a solicitar una pasantía y regresar a Estados Unidos.</p><p>La búsqueda de pasantías para estudiantes de psicología puede ser “increíblemente intensa para todos los estudiantes”, según Courtney Kibble, directora del programa de psicología en la Universidad Roosevelt.</p><p>Simplemente no hay suficientes pasantías para todos los candidatos de doctorado, y Kibble dijo que aproximadamente 600 estudiantes no lograron conseguir una.</p><p>Kibble comentó que la escasez se debe a un número creciente de estudiantes de doctorado y a un financiamiento limitado para los programas de pasantías. Fue aún más difícil para Barbosa, a pesar de su promedio alto, señaló Kibble, ya que un tercio de las pasantías no están disponibles para estudiantes internacionales.</p><p>“[Joabe] es un estudiante fenomenal en nuestro programa de Doctorado en Psicología”, dijo Kibble. “Para él, se convirtió en un juego de números en el que la discrepancia de disponibilidad de [pasantías] y la complicación de ser un estudiante internacional lo afectaron de manera negativa. Esto de ninguna manera refleja su preparación o disposición para completar una pasantía y obtener su doctorado”.</p><p>Barbosa se dirigió a su cuenta de Instagram, que ha acumulado una gran cantidad de seguidores, y se puso sentimental al anunciar la noticia mientras corría por la ciudad. Dijo que sus seguidores han sido extremadamente solidarios y algunos se han puesto en contacto para intentar ayudarlo.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div #F4F4F4; border-left: 8px solid transparent; transform: translateY(-4px) translateX(8px);"></div></div></div> <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; flex-grow: 1; justify-content: center; margin-bottom: 24px;"> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; margin-bottom: 6px; width: 224px;"></div> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div></div><p>Sin embargo, es poco probable que Barbosa pueda quedarse en su ciudad adoptiva.</p><p>“Las personas aquí me han tratado como un verdadero nativo de Chicago”, dijo Barbosa. “No tengo acento de Chicago y no he vivido aquí más que cinco años, pero la gente en Chicago me ha tratado como si hubiera estado aquí toda mi vida".</p><p>“Tal vez tenga que regresar a casa, pero llevo conmigo a Chicago. Amo esta ciudad”, agregó.</p><p>Barbosa dijo que desearía que hubiera más oportunidades para inmigrantes en Estados Unidos y alentó a otros estudiantes internacionales en situaciones similares a mantener la esperanza.</p><p>“Llegarán más oportunidades”, aseguró Barbosa. “Puede que no parezca así ahora, pero así será. Voy a hacer las cosas bien y no voy a quemar ningún puente”.</p><p>Después de lesionarse mientras practicaba senderismo en las montañas en agosto de 2024, Barbosa comenzó a correr como una forma de recuperarse.</p><p>Eso es lo que lo inspiró a convertirse en la primera persona en correr por cada una de las calles de Chicago. Ha completado poco más del 85% del mapa de Chicago, según la aplicación para correr City Strides.</p><p>“Ha sido asombroso”, dijo Barbosa, quien sale a correr con una bandera de Chicago atada como una capa. “No he estado aquí tanto tiempo, pero me ha encantado conocer cada vecindario y a la gente”.</p><p>Está en camino de completar el mapa en unas semanas y está planeando una carrera final el 14 de junio a las 10 a.m. Está invitando a todos los residentes de Chicago a unirse a él en una carrera por Michigan Avenue desde Oak St. hasta la Ida B. Wells Drive.</p><p>Barbosa también tiene el récord mundial Guinness por el tiempo más rápido conocido para visitar todas las estaciones de la Autoridad de Tránsito de Chicago (CTA), un esfuerzo que llevó a cabo con su amigo y hermano. Lo completaron en poco menos de 9 horas.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Venezuelan immigrant accused of killing Loyola freshman Sheridan Gorman now faces federal gun charge
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s top federal prosecutor said he’s taking “no chances”<b> </b>that Sheridan Gorman’s alleged killer will be released, filing an illegal gun possession charge<b> </b>Thursday<b> </b>against the Venezuelan immigrant already charged with the murder of the Loyola University Chicago freshman.</p><p>No federal court date has been scheduled for Jose Medina, 25,<b> </b>who was ordered detained Friday<b> </b>by a state court judge<b> </b>in connection with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/heres-what-to-know-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >Gorman’s slaying</a>. Medina’s attorney actually asked Cook County Circuit Court Judge D’Anthony Thedford<b> </b>to hold Medina in jail so he won’t be deported.</p><p>Now the feds have charged Medina with possessing<b> </b>a Smith & Wesson .40-caliber semiautomatic pistol while being unlawfully present in the United States, a charge carrying a maximum penalty of 10 years in prison.</p><p>“Given the senseless, cold-blooded nature of the murder of a young student with a bright future ahead of her, the Chicago U.S. attorney’s office will take no chances that this illegal alien perpetrator will be released back into our community,” U.S. Attorney Andrew S. Boutros said in a statement.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fe0000" name="image-fe0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" to death, Thursday, March 19, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5068012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5068012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6818x3826+0+606/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2F30%2F872fd020461881bad76323c2496f%2Frogersparkhomicide-260320-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago police officers investigate on the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach in Rogers Park hours after 18-year-old Loyola University student Sheridan Gorman was shot to death on March 19.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>An eight-page complaint alleges Chicago police found the gun in a Sheridan Road residence that was searched after video captured “a male in black clothing, wearing a black mask, walking with a distinct limp and slow gait” entering shortly after the<b> </b>March 19 shooting.</p><p>Witnesses described the shooter similarly.</p><p>An ATF special agent at the Crime Gun Intelligence Center in Chicago wrote that the gun turned out to be a high-probability match for the one used in Gorman’s slaying.</p><p>The federal complaint lays out how Medina was born in Venezuela and has “no lawful status in the United States.”<b> </b>It says a Border Patrol agent “encountered”<b> </b>Medina<b> </b>in the El Paso border sector on May 9, 2023. Medina was allegedly unable to provide a valid U.S. address but said he planned to reside in New Rochelle, New York.</p><p>Medina was released on his own recognizance on May 24, 2023, and was scheduled to appear for a hearing before an immigration judge in New York on Oct. 13, 2023, according to the complaint.</p><p>However, it says there “is no indication of any record of any further immigration related proceedings or adjustments in status.”</p><p>Gorman’s killing reignited the national debate over immigration and even drew the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" target="_blank" >attention of President Donald Trump</a>. Medina is accused of hiding at the end of a pier near Tobey Prinz Beach and shooting the 18-year-old Gorman<b> </b>in the back as she and her friends ran for cover.</p><p>Cook County Assistant Public Defender Julie Koehler has said that Medina <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/27/sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-jose-medina" target="_blank" >has a developmental disability</a> stemming from an earlier gunshot wound he suffered to his head during a robbery in Colombia. Medina had made his way to that country after fleeing violence in Venezuela, Koehler said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bc0000" name="image-bc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c06a0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3456x1940+0+1334/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F7f%2F134dfc4145f383e1cb01cc0bab53%2Fmedina-jose-d-o-b-04212000-date-of-photo-03262026-3.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Medina, Jose , photographed in detention." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c06a0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3456x1940+0+1334/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F7f%2F134dfc4145f383e1cb01cc0bab53%2Fmedina-jose-d-o-b-04212000-date-of-photo-03262026-3.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a90b337/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3456x1940+0+1334/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F7f%2F134dfc4145f383e1cb01cc0bab53%2Fmedina-jose-d-o-b-04212000-date-of-photo-03262026-3.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c06a0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3456x1940+0+1334/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F7f%2F134dfc4145f383e1cb01cc0bab53%2Fmedina-jose-d-o-b-04212000-date-of-photo-03262026-3.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jose Medina faces local and federal charges in the shooting death of Loyola University Chicago freshman Sheridan Gorman.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Cook County public defender’s office</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>He had to relearn how to walk and talk, and he has an indentation on the top of his head. He can’t read or write, Koehler said.</p><p>Medina wound up in Chicago in 2023 after reaching the U.S.-Mexico border in Texas and being detained there, according to Koehler. Although he asked to be deported back to Colombia, officials in Texas purportedly bused him to Chicago.</p><p>With no family here, Medina stayed in a migrant shelter and contracted tuberculosis that went undiagnosed, his attorney said. His mother eventually joined him here, and he was staying with her at an apartment building a block from where Gorman was killed.</p><p>Gorman’s family has said they “understand”<b> </b>the instinct for compassion.</p><p>“Every life has a story,” they said in a statement last week. “But we cannot lose sight of the simple, devastating truth at the center of all of this: Sheridan had a life too. There is a difference between understanding a life and excusing a loss.”</p><p><i>Contributing: David Struett, Sophie Sherry</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/heres-what-to-know-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >What to know about the killing of Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/27/sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-jose-medina" target="_blank" >Man charged with Loyola freshman’s murder has disability from gunshot to the head, attorney says</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" target="_blank" >Sheridan Gorman’s killing puts Chicago back at the center of America’s bitter fight over immigration</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Local officials warn of ICE arrests at domestic violence courthouse, decry feds violating state law
entries.summary: <p>Federal immigration agents are violating Illinois law and making arrests at Chicago-area courthouses, several local officials alleged at a press conference Thursday.</p><p>The Cook County Public Defender’s office said since late February, federal immigration agents have been spotted entering the Domestic Violence Courthouse at 555 W. Harrison St., or waiting directly outside.</p><p>In at least two of those instances, someone was arrested, including that of a woman <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/groups/378158360786470/posts/1530031978932430/" target="_blank" ><u>followed out of the courthouse</u></a> and detained by federal agents March 10. The other occurred February 27, officials said. Still another person was detained on March 18 at a different location, the Maywood courthouse.</p><p>The press conference was held hours after federal agents returned to the courthouse with “the intent to take custody of a person who was scheduled to appear for a hearing today,” the Cook County Public Defender’s office said. Officials said they weren’t aware of any arrests being made Thursday, but noted they don’t always immediately know when someone is taken.</p><p></p><p>“Survivors should be able to walk into a courthouse without fear [that] doing the right thing will put a target on their backs,” State Rep. Graciela Guzmán <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/ICIRR/videos/1656661488801326" target="_blank" ><u>said at Thursday’s press conference</u></a>. “This is contempt for our laws, our courts and survivors.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f02d21c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43d4b8f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/14c5806/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Sen. Graciela Guzmán, D-Chicago, speaks about recent Immigration and Customs enforcement during a press conference at the Cook County Offices at 118 S. Clark St. in the Loop on Thursday, April 2, 2026. Guzman represents the 20th District." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/14c5806/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d4ff143/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/14c5806/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Sen. Graciela Guzmán, D-Chicago, speaks about recent Immigration and Customs enforcement during a press conference at the Cook County Offices at 118 S. Clark St. in the Loop on Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Immigrations and Customs Enforcement and the Department of Homeland Security didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker signed a law in December banning federal agents from conducting civil immigration enforcement at state courthouses after the Chief Judge’s office <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/14/top-cook-county-judge-bars-civil-arrests-at-courthouses-citing-chilling-effect" >barred civil arrests at county courthouses</a> in October. Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle also signed an executive order <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/16/preckwinkle-issues-executive-order-to-create-ice-free-zones-in-cook-county" >prohibiting feds from staging on county property</a> as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/06/mayor-brandon-johnson-executive-order-city-owned-properties-ice-free-zones" >Chicago</a>, Evanston and others did the same.</p><p>Officials pointed to a pivot in federal immigration activity, away from neighborhoods where they <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/12/ice-chicago-albany-park-tear-gas" >faced heavy resistance</a>, and to places like courthouses and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/23/ice-agents-ohare-airport-partial-government-shutdown" >airports</a> where it can be more difficult for communities to respond.</p><p>Sharlyn Grace, deputy public defender for policy at the Cook County Public Defender’s office, said reports of federal agents using county property to stage had fallen off since Preckwinkle’s order banning them from doing so, which she said was a good sign for the legislative tools officials did have.</p><p>However, the law they alleged federal agents were violating doesn't carry criminal penalties; it requires the person detained to file a civil lawsuit — something she admitted is already a challenge for those detained by the feds.</p><p>“It’s one of our only tools,” Grace said. “But it’s obviously limited by a person in immigration custody to access a lawyer.”</p><p>Maralea Negron, vice president of policy and impact at The Network: Advocating Against Domestic Violence, said having Border Patrol and ICE agents at courthouses would have a “chilling effect” on domestic violence survivors working with the justice system.</p><p>“Our society tells survivors to report to law enforcement and go to the courts, and yet when they do, they and their families can be taken,” Negron said. “The presence of ICE makes the decision to leave an abusive relationship or household even harder.”</p><div entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Felony case dropped against DePaul student accused of spitting on ICE agent at Chicago's O'Hare Airport
entries.summary: <p>Cook County’s top prosecutor abruptly dropped a felony case against a DePaul University student accused of spitting on an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent over the weekend at O’Hare Airport.</p><p>In an arrest report, Chicago police said Brett Heier “intentionally and forcefully” <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/01/ice-trump-depaul-spitting-chicago-ohare-crime" target="_blank" >spat on the ICE agent</a> Sunday while leaving Terminal 2 at O’Hare. Heier, 20, was charged with felony aggravated battery to a peace officer and was ordered released pending trial during his first court appearance Wednesday. </p><p>By Thursday evening, Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen O’Neill Burke had “personally reviewed the case and directed prosecutors to dismiss the charge,” according to a statement released by her office. The unusual move comes as O’Neill Burke faces mounting calls to investigate and prosecute immigration agents who carried out an aggressive deportation campaign in the Chicago area.</p><p>The statement didn't provide an explanation for dismissing the case, saying only that O'Neill Burke's office "continually evaluates evidence and reviews cases presented to us by law enforcement to ensure charging decisions are appropriate."</p><p>Heier's lawyer, Adam Sheppard, had insisted in an interview with the Sun-Times that the case was overblown. Sheppard argued Heier was only accused of spitting on the agent's shoe, and it may have been unintentional.</p><p>“It is comforting to know that the Cook County State’s Attorney’s Office will exercise proper prosecutorial discretion,” Sheppard said after the case was dropped.</p><p>The alleged spitting incident at O’Hare came a week after President Donald Trump announced he was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/23/ice-agents-ohare-airport-partial-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >sending ICE agents to airports</a> across the country to assist with security amid the fight over funding for the Department of Homeland Security.</p><p>The government shutdown resulting from that funding fight has led to long lines at airports and staffing issues among Transportation Security Administration agents, who went unpaid from mid-February until Trump signed an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/travel/2026/03/26/tsa-travel-airports-dhs-ice-ohare-federal-budget-congress-impasse-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >executive order giving them back-pay</a> last week.</p><p>Employees of other DHS agencies, including ICE and Customs and Border Protection, have continued to earn paychecks because they’re being funded by Trump’s domestic policy bill passed over the summer.</p><p>The shutdown was prompted by Democrats’ unanswered calls for reforms after two U.S. citizens were fatally shot during an immigration enforcement operation in Minnesota earlier this year.</p><p>O'Neill Burke has said she’s legally handcuffed when it comes to targeting the feds who carried out the earlier campaign in the Chicago area — even as legal efforts have been launched to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-cook-county-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor-oneill-burke-loevy" target="_blank" >install a special prosecutor</a> in her office to do just that.</p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Man sentenced to 55 years in prison for 2023 murder of Chicago police officer Aréanah Preston
entries.summary: <p>A man was sentenced to 55 years in prison Thursday after he pleaded guilty to the 2023 murder of off-duty Chicago police officer Aréanah Preson in Avalon Park.</p><p>Joseph Brooks, 22, pleaded guilty to one county of felony first-degree murder, court records show.</p><p>Brooks and three others were charged with first-degree murder in May 2023, less than a week after Preston was fatally shot in an attempted robbery near her home. She was off-duty but still in her police uniform.</p><p>Trevell Breeland, Jakwon Buchanan and Jaylan Frazier’s cases are still pending.</p><p>Preston, 24, had worked for the police department for three years and was most recently assigned to the Calumet District. At the time of the shooting, she was preparing for her graduation from Loyola University Chicago with a master’s degree of Jurisprudence.</p><p>After her death, her dad told the Sun-Times his daughter was “trying to make a change on this Earth.”</p><p>Prosecutors said the shooting took place after a long, violent robbery spree that began after Buchanan’s girlfriend told him “she needed money for a barbecue.”</p><p>Brooks told detectives he shot Preston when he saw her reaching for her gun, prosecutors said in 2023.<br></p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Marshall High School boys basketball team's state championship brings pride to West Side
entries.summary: <p>When the boys' basketball team at John Marshall Metropolitan High School <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/2026/03/14/marshall-basketball-ihsa-state-goreville" >took home the Class 1A state title</a> last month, it was bigger than just one school.</p><p>“This championship wasn’t just for us, it was for the West Side of Chicago,” head coach Darrin Laye said Thursday afternoon, when the squad was honored with a celebration at the school.</p><p>Laye credited the victory, the school's first basketball championship since 2008, to the players’ hard work and dedication. Marshall won the 3A title in 2008 and also captured state championships in 1958 and 1960.</p><p>“These kids deserve it,” said Laye.</p><p>Students, faculty, parents and even alumni packed the auditorium of the East Garfield Park school, welcoming the team with applause and cheers.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure They’re like my brothers.”</p><p>The Marshall Commandos came from 10 points down to defeat Goreville 52-44 at the State Farm Center in Champaign.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ed0000" name="image-ed0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23d022c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e2db9c3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44dddd9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Darrin Laye, head coach of the boys' basketball team at John Marshall Metropolitan High School, addresses the crowd during a celebration for the team's state title. The event was held in the auditorium of the high school, 3250 W. Adams St. in East Garfield Park on Thursday, April 2, 2026. The team won the won the Illinois High School Association Class 1A state championship in March." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44dddd9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6a74658/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44dddd9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F7f%2F403cab1b49cb8a706d2f1eafd5ea%2Fmarshallbasketball-coach-260403-5.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Darrin Laye, head coach of the boys’ basketball team at John Marshall Metropolitan High School, addresses the crowd during a celebration on Thursday for the team’s state title. The event was held in the auditorium of the high school, 3250 W. Adams St. in East Garfield Park. The team won the won the Illinois High School Association Class 1A state championship in March.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>After the win, Laye said he finally felt a sense of relief after a long season. “It’s like I can breathe now,” he said. “We can breathe now.”</p><p>But their victory wouldn’t have been possible without the support of their families and the rest of the school community.</p><p>“The minute you step through the doors of this school, you feel the love,” said Laye.</p><p>For Gibson and fellow senior Amari Cornell, Thursday’s celebration marked the end of their high school basketball careers, and both said they’re proud to have helped bring a championship trophy back home.</p><p>But Cornell urged his younger teammates to return next school year ready to work hard. “Come back better and do what we did this year, but harder,” the 18-year-old said.</p><p>Junior Antonio Archer said he was ready to take on the challenge. “We’ll bring the trophy back next year,” he said.</p><p>Also attending the celebration to offer their congratulations were Mayor Brandon Johnson, Ald. Jason Ervin, state Rep. La Shawn Ford and U.S. Rep Danny Davis.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-370000" name="image-370000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/32aa1b7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6070x3407+0+320/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F71%2F0f%2Fefa932414b5bb1bc4ba2920b59e2%2Fmarshallbasketball-260403-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55237b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6070x3407+0+320/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F71%2F0f%2Fefa932414b5bb1bc4ba2920b59e2%2Fmarshallbasketball-260403-11.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e552a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6070x3407+0+320/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F71%2F0f%2Fefa932414b5bb1bc4ba2920b59e2%2Fmarshallbasketball-260403-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson greets a member of the Marshall Commandos during a celebration for them at John Marshall Metropolitan High School in East Garfield Park, Thursday, April 2, 2026. The team won the won the Illinois High School Association Class 1A state championship in March.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“You all did not just represent class and sophistication on the basketball court, but as great students and as young brothers on the west side of Chicago, I am proud of each and every one of you,” said Johnson.</p><p>Johnson also praised the dedication of the school’s teachers and administrators.</p><p>“You're setting these students up to be the next leaders,” he said.</p><p>Marshall's victory was the first time a Chicago Public League school captured a championship in the state’s smallest class.</p><p>When the IHSA tournament expanded to two classes in 1972, the Public League was granted an automatic bid to the Class AA state finals, making it impossible for its teams to compete in Class A. The tournament later expanded to four classes in 2008, and this season marked the first time a Public League team advanced to the Class 1A title game.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center>
